details: A peddler said the children love it. The adults complain about carrying tables but show up every time.
He said the food is nothing special, but the conversation makes it better.
reactions: A shared morning sets the tone for the whole day. Something to be said for starting together.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a stretch of path where the ground buzzes underfoot. Some kind of insect nest below the surface.
details: A traveler said it's unnerving. You feel the vibration through your boots before you hear it.
People there walk around it out of habit. Visitors usually stop and stare.
reactions: I'd walk around it too. Anything buzzing under the ground can stay there.

statCheck.pass: Underground hives like that are usually burrowing wasps. Harmless unless you dig them up, but the vibration means the colony is large.
statCheck.fail: I don't know insects well enough to guess what's down there.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said their best weaver went blind in one eye. Still weaves, just slower.
details: He said the work is still beautiful. You'd never know from the finished cloth.
The weaver won't stop. Says the hands remember even when the eye doesn't.
reactions: Skill that deep doesn't leave you. It lives in the muscle, not the sight.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} dug a new latrine pit and hit a pocket of foul-smelling gas. Cleared the area for a day.
details: A carter said people were gagging from fifty paces away. Eyes watering, the works.
They filled it in and dug somewhere else. Nobody argued about the new location.
reactions: Sometimes the ground tells you where not to dig. You just have to listen with your nose.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has an older couple who sit outside every evening and wave at everyone who passes. Every evening, without fail.
details: A peddler said it's the first thing you see arriving and the last thing leaving. Friendly pair.
They've been doing it so long that people there feel uneasy if the bench is empty.
reactions: Small comforts like that define a place more than any building or field.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} tried to build a second oven and it collapsed before they could fire it. Mortar wasn't right.
details: He said the builder was mortified. Weeks of work in a pile of rubble.
They're going to try again, but this time they're asking someone with experience to supervise.
reactions: Building looks simple until you try it. There's a reason experienced hands cost more.

statCheck.pass: Clay mortar needs to cure slowly before the first firing. If they rushed the drying, the thermal shock would crack everything loose.
statCheck.fail: I've never built an oven. Sounds harder than it looks.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a young girl who climbs every tall thing in sight. Roofs, trees, fence posts. Fearless.
details: A trader said the parents have given up trying to stop her. She's too quick.
Half the settlement watches her with dread and the other half cheers her on.
reactions: Every place has a climber. They either grow out of it or get very good at it.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} traded a full cart of {resource_type} for a pair of breeding geese. Seems like a lot.
details: A peddler said the geese are supposed to be a rare breed. Bigger eggs, more meat, hardier in winter.
Whether that's true or just good salesmanship, the seller got the better end of that deal for now.
reactions: Time will tell if those geese were worth it. Breeding stock is a gamble either way.

statCheck.pass: Good breeding stock pays for itself in two seasons if managed right. The real question is whether they know how to raise geese.
statCheck.fail: I don't know geese from ducks. A bird's a bird to me.

entries.intro: A carter told me {other_settlement} had a thunderstorm so loud it spooked every animal in the settlement. Chaos for an hour.
details: He said a goat kicked through a fence, a horse broke its tether, and the chickens didn't lay for two days after.
The people were fine. The animals took a week to settle down.
reactions: Animals remember storms longer than people do. Can't reason them out of fear.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a blackberry thicket that produces more fruit than they can eat or preserve. It's a problem of abundance.
details: A merchant said the birds get half of it and the wasps get a quarter. What's left is still more than enough.
They've been trading the surplus, but the berries don't travel well. Soft fruit, rough roads.
reactions: Too much of a good thing is still a good thing. There are worse problems to have.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a well where the winch squeals so loud you can hear it across the whole settlement.
details: A traveler said everyone knows when someone's drawing water. It's the settlement's alarm clock.
They could oil it, but at this point it's become part of the daily rhythm. Nobody bothers.
reactions: Familiar sounds stop being annoying after long enough. They just become home.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has a cellar that stays cool enough to keep butter solid all summer. Dug into the right kind of stone.
details: He said people store their perishables together down there. A communal cold room, essentially.
It's changed how they eat in summer. Dairy lasts, meat holds longer, nothing spoils as fast.
reactions: A good cellar is worth more than a good roof, and I don't say that lightly.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has someone who snores so loud the neighbors have complained to the elder. Formally.
details: A trader said the elder didn't know what to do with the complaint. There's no rule against snoring.
The neighbors ended up stuffing cloth in the wall gaps. The snorer sleeps peacefully, unaware.
reactions: Living close to other people means hearing other people. That's the deal.

entries.intro: I heard a widow in {other_settlement} has been maintaining her husband's workshop exactly as he left it. Every tool in its place.
details: A merchant said she dusts the bench and sharpens the blades even though nobody uses them.
People there respect it. Nobody's asked her to clear the space for someone else.
reactions: Grief takes its own shape. If keeping a workshop tidy is hers, who's to say otherwise.

statCheck.pass: She's not keeping a workshop. She's keeping a memory alive. The day she lets it go will be harder than the day she lost him.
statCheck.fail: People mourn in their own way. It's not for me to judge.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} has been getting colder winters than they used to. People there swear it wasn't this harsh ten years ago.
details: He said the older folks remember milder seasons. The younger ones think this is just how it is.
They've been insulating buildings more and stockpiling extra firewood. Adapting.
reactions: Weather changes over years and you don't notice until suddenly you do.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} makes the best leather belts any trader has seen. Thick, even, and they don't stretch.
details: A carter said he bought one three years ago and it still looks new. Better than anything sold here.
The leatherworker there apparently picks only the best hides and rejects anything with a flaw.
reactions: Quality takes patience. Most people don't have enough of either.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a fence post that keeps falling over no matter how many times they reset it. Same post, same spot.
details: A peddler said the ground there must have a pocket of soft soil or a buried root. Nothing holds.
At this point it's become a running joke. People make bets on how long it'll stay up.
reactions: Some battles aren't worth winning. Move the fence post six inches and call it done.

entries.intro: A traveler told me {other_settlement} collects feathers from every bird that nests nearby. Uses them for stuffing pillows and bedding.
details: He said the pillows there are softer than anything he's slept on. Proper luxury for a settlement that size.
They trade the surplus feathers too. Light to carry, easy to sell.
reactions: Using what's around you is the oldest kind of wealth. No cost but effort.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had an argument about whether soup counts as a meal or a drink. It got heated.
details: A trader said the debate started at dinner and went on for three days. Families took sides.
They never resolved it. People just stopped talking about it and moved on.
reactions: When there's nothing real to argue about, people will invent something. Human nature.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a late frost that killed half their seedlings. Weeks of planting gone overnight.
details: A merchant said the farmers there were devastated. Some of them just stood in the fields staring.
They replanted what they could, but the season's shorter now. It'll be a lean year.
reactions: Frost doesn't care about your plans. One cold night and the whole season shifts.

statCheck.pass: A late frost usually hits low-lying fields first. Cold air sinks. The people with plots on higher ground probably kept most of their crop.
statCheck.fail: Bad luck with weather. Not much anyone can do about it.

entries.intro: A peddler mentioned {other_settlement} has a man who can identify any wood by smell alone. Blindfolded, doesn't matter.
details: He said the man picks up a piece, sniffs it once, and tells you the tree, the age, and whether it was cut wet or dry.
People bring him scraps just to test him. He's never been wrong that anyone recalls.
reactions: A lifetime of working with wood does that. Knowledge gets into your senses.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a drainage ditch that breeds frogs every spring. Hundreds of them, all singing at once.
details: A carter said the noise is deafening at dusk. You have to shout to be heard near the ditch.
The children catch them and the adults step on them by accident. Nobody's winning that fight.
reactions: Frogs are a sign of clean water, at least. Silver lining to the chorus.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} started marking every building with the family name of who built it. Carved right into the lintel.
details: A traveler said you can trace the whole history of the place by reading the names on the beams.
Some names appear on half the buildings. Those families built the bones of the settlement.
reactions: Putting your name on your work is honest pride. I wish more people did it.

entries.intro: A trader told me {other_settlement} has a woman who won't let anyone sit in her chair at communal meals. It's her chair.
details: He said she arrives early every time just to claim it. Nobody dares try it when she's late.
The chair itself is nothing special. Wooden, worn, same as every other one.
reactions: People attach to the strangest things. But honestly, I get it. Everyone wants something that's just theirs.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a batch of candles that burned with a green flame. Nobody could explain it.
details: A peddler said the tallow must have had copper dust mixed in from the rendering pot. That's all it takes.
People there were spooked until someone figured out the cause. Then they thought it was funny.
reactions: Everything has an explanation if you look hard enough. Most mysteries are just chemistry.

statCheck.pass: Copper salts in tallow produce a green flame. Whoever rendered the fat used a copper vessel and didn't clean it well enough.
statCheck.fail: Candles are candles. I don't know why they'd burn green.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has been losing young people to the bigger towns. Three left this season alone.
details: A merchant said the ones who leave rarely come back. Better pay, more people their age, more to do.
The older folks there try not to take it personally, but you can see it weighs on them.
reactions: Small places bleed people slowly. It's nobody's fault, but it's nobody's solution either.

statCheck.pass: When the young ones leave, the settlement ages fast. Within a few years the workload falls on fewer and older shoulders. That's how places fade.
statCheck.fail: People go where they go. Not much to say about it.

entries.intro: A carter said {other_settlement} has the best-stacked woodpiles he's seen. Straight edges, perfect gaps for airflow, no wasted space.
details: He said one man there taught everyone else his method. Now the whole settlement stacks the same way.
It dries faster and takes up less room. Simple technique, big difference.
reactions: Good habits spread when the results are obvious. Nobody needs convincing when the wood dries in half the time.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a rooster that crows at midnight instead of dawn. Nobody can fix it.
details: A peddler said they've tried covering the coop, moving it farther away, everything. The bird doesn't care.
Half the settlement has adjusted their sleep around one confused rooster.
reactions: One stubborn animal can rearrange an entire settlement's routine. There's a lesson in that.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a creek that freezes so thick in winter you can drive a loaded cart across it.
details: A merchant said it becomes their best shortcut for two months of the year. Saves a long loop around.
Come spring, the ice breaks up fast. You need to know the exact day to stop crossing.
reactions: Nature gives and takes on its own schedule. Smart people pay attention to the calendar.

statCheck.pass: Ice thick enough for a cart needs a full month of hard frost. If the winter's mild, people test it too early and learn the hard way.
statCheck.fail: I don't trust ice. If I can't see the bottom, I go around.

entries.intro: A traveler told me {other_settlement} has the quietest mornings he's ever experienced. No animals, no wind, just silence.
details: He said it's eerie at first. Then it's peaceful. Then you don't want to leave.
The settlement sits in a sheltered spot where the wind breaks before it reaches them.
reactions: Quiet is underrated. Most of us don't realize how noisy our days are until the noise stops.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} found weevils in their flour stores. Had to throw out half of it.
details: A trader said they caught it early enough to save the rest, but it was a close call.
Now they check every sack twice and keep bay leaves in the stores. Lesson learned the expensive way.
reactions: Weevils are quiet thieves. By the time you see one, there are a hundred you don't.

statCheck.pass: Bay leaves repel weevils, but the real trick is freezing the grain for a night before storing it. Kills the eggs before they hatch.
statCheck.fail: I check my flour. That's about all I know to do.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a man who carves spoons from every type of wood he can find. Has a whole collection.
details: A peddler said the man swears each wood gives the food a different taste. Most people think he's imagining it.
He's got over thirty spoons and uses a different one depending on the meal.
reactions: Harmless obsessions make life interesting. At least spoons are useful.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} had their entire onion crop come up tiny this year. Bulbs the size of marbles.
details: He said they planted at the wrong time. Too late, not enough growing days before the heat set in.
They're using the tiny onions anyway. A small onion is still an onion.
reactions: Timing is everything with planting. A week off and you get marbles instead of onions.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a tradition of the youngest person in town ringing the morning bell. It's considered an honor.
details: A carter said the current bell-ringer is about eight years old and takes it deadly seriously.
When a new baby is born, the bell passes to the next youngest. It's a whole ceremony.
reactions: Giving children responsibility early builds character. Or at least punctuality.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a path that floods ankle-deep with warm water after it rains. Some kind of underground spring mixing in.
details: A merchant said people there don't mind it. Children splash through it on purpose.
Visitors are baffled. Warm puddles aren't something you expect on a dirt path.
reactions: Every place has its oddities. Warm puddles are a better one than most.

statCheck.pass: A warm spring feeding into surface runoff means there's geothermal heat below the surface. Useful if they ever think to pipe it.
statCheck.fail: Warm water coming out of the ground is strange, but the ground is full of strange things.

entries.intro: A peddler told me {other_settlement} has been exporting {resource_type} at prices that undercut everyone else. Traders are annoyed.
details: He said they must have found a richer source or a faster way to process it. Either way, the market's shifted.
Some settlements are grumbling about it but nobody's found a way to compete yet.
reactions: Cheap supply is good for buyers and bad for everyone who was selling at the old price.

statCheck.pass: Undercutting usually means they're selling volume over quality. The {resource_type} might be cheaper, but it won't be as good. Give it time.
statCheck.fail: I don't follow trade prices closely enough to say.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} built a communal bench right at the highest point near their settlement. Best view in the area, they say.
details: A traveler said people go up there after work just to sit and look out. Nobody talks much, they just watch.
He said the sunset from that bench was worth the walk up.
reactions: A place to sit and think is worth more than people realize. Not everything useful produces something.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a problem with slugs eating their greens. Every morning, fresh holes in every leaf.
details: A carter said the gardeners there have tried ash, salt, crushed shells. Nothing works for long.
They've accepted it and just plant more than they need. The slugs get their share.
reactions: Sometimes the answer isn't defeating the problem but planting around it. Acceptance over combat.

statCheck.pass: Slugs avoid copper. A strip of copper around the garden beds would keep them out, but copper isn't cheap or easy to come by.
statCheck.fail: Slugs are slow, persistent, and apparently unstoppable. I don't have advice.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has an old woman who predicts the harvest yield by watching how the crows behave in spring.
details: He said she's been right more often than not over the last ten years. People have stopped laughing at her.
Her method is watching how high they nest and how early they start gathering. Crude, but it correlates.
reactions: Animals know things we don't. Watching them closely is its own kind of wisdom.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has twins who finish each other's sentences. Whole conversations where you can't tell who's talking.
details: A trader said it's charming for the first minute and maddening after five.
The twins think it's perfectly normal. Everyone else finds it unsettling.
reactions: Twins have their own rules. The rest of us just live alongside it.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} put up a windbreak along their north side. Branches woven between posts, nothing fancy, but it works.
details: A peddler said the wind used to howl through there in winter. Now it just murmurs.
Took them three days to build and it's the best thing they've done for comfort in years.
reactions: The simplest fixes are often the ones people put off the longest. Three days for years of relief.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said their only ladder broke and nobody can reach the upper storage anymore.
details: He said they've been stacking crates and climbing. It's not safe, but it's what they've got.
Building a new ladder sounds easy until you need straight timber and the right tools. Neither is handy.
reactions: You never appreciate a ladder until you're standing at the bottom without one.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a man who hums the same tune all day, every day. Nobody knows where he learned it.
details: A merchant said you can hear him before you see him. It's become his signature.
Some people find it soothing. Others find it maddening. There's no in between.
reactions: A person's habits become the background noise of a place. You miss them when they're gone.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a season where their carrots came up purple instead of orange. Same seeds, different color.
details: A peddler said the taste was the same, maybe even sweeter. But people refused to eat purple carrots at first.
Now they've gotten used to it and some even prefer them. Funny how that works.
reactions: People resist change even when the change is harmless. A carrot is a carrot, purple or not.

entries.intro: A carter told me {other_settlement} has a family that keeps the cleanest house anyone's seen. You could eat off their floor.
details: He said even their children wipe their feet without being told. It's instinct at this point.
The rest of the settlement finds it impressive and slightly intimidating.
reactions: There's clean, and then there's whatever that family is doing. I admire it from a safe distance.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} trained a dog to fetch specific tools by name. Point at the bench and say the word, the dog brings it.
details: A merchant said the dog knows at least ten tools. Hammer, awl, chisel, plane. All of them.
The owner claims it took years. Everyone else claims the dog is smarter than most people.
reactions: A smart dog is worth three hired hands. And it doesn't eat as much.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had their meeting hall door swell shut in the humidity. Couldn't open it for two days.
details: A traveler said they held meetings outside until someone planed the edge down. Problem solved.
But every humid stretch since, they check the door first. Once bitten.
reactions: Wood and weather have their own relationship. You just adapt to it.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has a child who asks every visitor the same question. Where did you sleep last night?
details: He said the child is genuinely curious about how other people live. Not suspicious, just fascinated.
Most visitors find it charming. A few find it oddly personal from a six-year-old.
reactions: Children ask the questions adults are too polite for. That's half their value.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} cured a whole batch of hides so well that the leather is still supple years later. Best tanning anyone's seen.
details: A trader said they used bark from a tree that only grows near their settlement. Something in the tannin.
Leatherworkers elsewhere have tried to replicate it and can't quite match the result.
reactions: Local knowledge is the kind you can't buy. It comes from generations of trial and error.

statCheck.pass: Different bark produces different tannin. Oak gives firmness, willow gives flexibility. Whatever they're using gives both, which is rare.
statCheck.fail: Tanning is an art. I just wear the leather.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a visitor who taught the children a clapping game. Now it's all they do.
details: A merchant said you can hear the rhythm from every corner. Clap-clap-slap, all day long.
The adults are losing their patience but the children are obsessed.
reactions: Children's fads are intense and mercifully short. Give it two weeks.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has a field of {resource_type} that hasn't been harvested yet because they're arguing about who owns it.
details: He said the crop's going to rot if they don't decide soon. Two families both claim they planted it.
Meanwhile the field sits there, ready, and everyone just looks at it.
reactions: Pride is an expensive crop to grow. It doesn't feed anyone.

statCheck.pass: Ownership disputes over a field usually come from unclear agreements at planting time. Verbal deals without witnesses are worth the paper they're not written on.
statCheck.fail: Land disputes are above my head. I just hope they harvest before it rots.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a grandmother who tells a different bedtime story every night. Never repeats.
details: A traveler said she's been doing it for years. The children won't sleep without a new one.
He asked her where the stories come from. She just tapped her head and smiled.
reactions: A good memory and a good imagination together is a rare gift. Those children are lucky.

entries.intro: I heard the food stores in {other_settlement} got raided by a bold fox that chewed through the door. Ate well and left.
details: A peddler said the fox took cheese, dried meat, and ignored the grain entirely. Discerning palate.
They reinforced the door with metal strips. The fox hasn't been back, but they're not taking chances.
reactions: A fox that picks its meals is a fox that knows what it's doing. I'd reinforce the door too.

entries.intro: A carter said {other_settlement} has a system for sharing tools. You sign them out and bring them back by sundown.
details: He said it works surprisingly well. Most people return things on time.
The few who don't have their name posted on a board for everyone to see. Peer pressure is effective.
reactions: Shared tools only work if everyone respects the system. Sounds like they do.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a chimney that catches fire at least once a season. Same chimney, same house.
details: A trader said the owner won't clean it properly. Everyone else has told them, but they wave it off.
One of these days it'll take the whole building. The neighbors are nervous.
reactions: Stubbornness about maintenance always costs more in the end.

statCheck.pass: Chimney fires are creosote buildup. Thick, tarry residue from burning unseasoned wood. The fix is simple. Burn dry wood and clean the flue twice a year.
statCheck.fail: Fire is fire. Keep your chimney clean, that's all I know.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a seamstress who can look at someone once and cut fabric to fit them without measuring. Just knows.
details: A merchant said her work fits better than clothes that were actually measured. It's uncanny.
She says it's not a gift, just years of practice. Nobody believes her.
reactions: Mastery looks like magic to people who haven't put in the time. It's always just practice.

entries.intro: A traveler said {other_settlement} had two neighbors swap houses because each preferred the other's view. Just traded.
details: He said the elder approved it without argument. Both families happier, no cost to anyone.
The rest of the settlement thought it was ridiculous. Then they admitted it was smart.
reactions: Simple solutions to simple problems. If only everything were that easy.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has been plagued by wasps nesting in their eaves. Every building, same problem.
details: A peddler said people eat indoors all summer to avoid them. Outdoor meals are impossible.
They knock the nests down and new ones appear within days. Persistent insects.
reactions: Wasps are nature's way of reminding you who was here first.

statCheck.pass: Wasps nest where there's exposed wood with the right grain for scraping. A coat of oil or paint on the eaves would make the wood useless to them.
statCheck.fail: I hate wasps. That's my full contribution to this topic.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has an agreement with a nearby settlement to watch each other's herds during festivals. Trade of favors.
details: A trader said it lets both places celebrate properly without worrying about the animals.
The arrangement's been going for years. Neither side has let the other down.
reactions: Trust between settlements is worth more than any trade agreement. This is how you build it.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} has a sandpit where the children play that's turned into the muddiest spot in the settlement.
details: He said every child there has permanent dirt under their fingernails. The parents have given up.
The pit was supposed to be temporary. That was three years ago.
reactions: Give children mud and they'll be happy forever. Give them clean clothes and they'll fix that fast.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} tried brewing a new kind of drink from dried fruit and honey. Results were mixed.
details: A carter said half the batch was delicious and the other half tasted like vinegar. Same recipe, same pot.
They're trying again, but with less confidence this time.
reactions: Fermentation has a mind of its own. You can follow the recipe and still get vinegar.

statCheck.pass: Temperature controls fermentation. The batches that went sour were probably stored in a warmer spot. Even a few degrees changes the outcome.
statCheck.fail: I drink what I'm handed and don't ask questions about the process.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has an echo near their settlement that repeats every shout twice. The children love it.
details: A peddler said you can hear them yelling at the hillside all afternoon. Names, nonsense words, just to hear them back.
The adults tolerate it because the echo keeps the children occupied and out of trouble.
reactions: Free entertainment courtesy of geography. Can't beat the price.

entries.intro: A peddler told me {other_settlement} has a woman who barters fiercely for every transaction. Not a single coin changes hands without negotiation.
details: He said she once argued over the price of a turnip for twenty minutes. And won.
Traders either respect her or avoid her. Nobody walks away feeling they got the better deal.
reactions: A hard bargainer protects the whole settlement's interests. I'd rather have her on my side than not.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} lost a whole row of saplings to a windstorm. Snapped them clean at the base.
details: A trader said they'd just planted them that spring. Months of work gone in one night.
They replanted immediately. Nothing else to do but start over.
reactions: Starting over is the hardest kind of patience. Most people quit before the second try.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has an old millstone that someone turned into a table. Sits right in the middle of the settlement.
details: A merchant said people eat on it, play games on it, lay out goods on it. It's become the center of everything.
The stone's surface is worn smooth from decades of grain. Now it's smooth from decades of elbows.
reactions: Repurposing something old into something useful is a kind of respect for what came before.

entries.intro: A carter mentioned that {other_settlement} has a well with the sweetest water he's tasted. Cold, clean, and slightly mineral.
details: He said he filled every waterskin he had before leaving. Worth the detour.
The people there don't think it's special. They've been drinking it all their lives.
reactions: You never appreciate what you have when it's all you've known. Takes an outsider to notice.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has had three different people try to organize their storage room and all three gave up.
details: A peddler said every system lasts about a week before people go back to piling things wherever they fit.
The storage room has layers. Each organizer's system buried under the next person's chaos.
reactions: Organizing a communal space is a war nobody wins. Entropy always has the numbers.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has started salting their paths in winter with ash from the communal fire. Keeps the ice down.
details: A traveler said the paths are dark gray all winter but nobody slips. A fair trade.
They sweep the ash off in spring and work it into the garden beds. Nothing wasted.
reactions: Using what you already have to solve a problem is the smartest kind of resourcefulness.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} has a man who counts everything. People, animals, stones in a wall. Can't help himself.
details: He said the man knows exactly how many fence posts, roof beams, and water buckets the settlement owns.
People find it odd but occasionally useful. When something goes missing, he's the first to know.
reactions: Everyone has their thing. His just happens to have a practical upside.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a goat that learned to open the latch on the food store. They've changed the latch three times.
details: A trader said the goat watches people operate the latch, then copies them. Smart animal.
They finally put the latch high enough that the goat can't reach. For now.
reactions: Never underestimate a goat with motivation. They're smarter than they look.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has the longest continuous hedge anyone's ever walked past. Runs along the entire approach.
details: A carter said it's trimmed flat on top and straight on the sides. Years of patient work.
It breaks the wind, marks the boundary, and looks impressive. Triple purpose.
reactions: A good hedge is architecture that grows itself. All it costs is patience and a sharp blade.

entries.intro: A traveler told me {other_settlement} had a batch of pottery explode in the kiln. Shards everywhere, weeks of work destroyed.
details: He said the potter forgot to dry the pots long enough before firing. Moisture trapped inside turned to steam.
The kiln itself survived, but the potter's pride didn't.
reactions: Shortcuts in craft always announce themselves eventually. Usually loudly.

statCheck.pass: Air pockets and moisture in clay turn to steam in the kiln. The explosion means they rushed the drying. Two more days would have saved the batch.
statCheck.fail: Clay and kilns are beyond me. I just use the finished pots.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a tradition of leaving a small offering of food at the edge of their settlement every evening. For luck, they say.
details: A peddler said the food is always gone by morning. Probably animals, but nobody says that out loud.
The tradition is older than anyone living there. Nobody remembers who started it or why.
reactions: Traditions don't need explanations to have value. The doing is the point.

statCheck.pass: Leaving food out attracts wildlife to the edge of the settlement, which keeps it away from the stores. Whether they know it or not, the tradition works.
statCheck.fail: If it makes them feel better, who am I to judge? I've got my own habits that don't make sense.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} got a delivery of {resource_type} so good that the whole settlement stopped work to look at it.
details: A merchant said the quality was unlike anything they'd seen before. Clean, consistent, no flaws.
They're trying to establish a regular supply, but the source is far away and the price is steep.
reactions: Good materials change what's possible. One delivery like that can raise the standard for years.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has a couple that argues about everything in public and is apparently devoted to each other in private.
details: He said the shouting matches are legendary. Whole settlement hears them, nobody intervenes.
A neighbor told him they hold hands every evening after the arguing's done. That's just how they are.
reactions: Every couple has their system. If it works for them, the rest of us can mind our business.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a young man who can split a log in one swing. Clean through, every time.
details: A carter said he watched the man split a whole cord in less than an hour. Methodical and powerful.
People bring him their toughest logs. He seems to enjoy the challenge.
reactions: Strength applied well is a pleasure to watch. Raw power is nothing without technique.

entries.intro: I heard the path from here to {other_settlement} has a stretch where the wildflowers are so thick you can barely see the dirt.
details: A merchant said it only lasts a few weeks in spring, but it's worth timing a trip for.
The colors change every year depending on the rain. This year was mostly blue and yellow.
reactions: Beauty that comes and goes is more precious for the going. Permanent beauty stops being noticed.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said their communal meals have gotten better since they started rotating who seasons the pot.
details: He said different cooks bring different flavors. One night it's peppery, the next it's herbed, the next it's plain.
People look forward to certain cooks' nights. It's become a quiet source of pride.
reactions: Variety in small things keeps daily life from going stale. Even food gets boring without it.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a shed roof that's slowly turning green with moss. Looks like a hill from a distance.
details: A peddler said it keeps the rain off better than any thatch he's seen. The moss seals every gap.
They didn't plant it on purpose. The moss just grew and they had the sense to leave it.
reactions: Nature does its own repairs if you let it. Not always, but when it works, it works beautifully.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a problem with their bread not rising. Three batches in a row, flat as boards.
details: A merchant said the baker blames the flour. The miller blames the baker. Nobody's admitting fault.
In the meantime, people are eating flatbread and pretending it's intentional.
reactions: When the bread goes wrong, the whole settlement feels it. Bread's the mood of a place.

statCheck.pass: Flat bread usually means the yeast is dead. If they stored it somewhere too warm, the culture would have died. They need a fresh starter.
statCheck.fail: I eat bread, I don't bake it. Can't help with this one.

entries.intro: A carter said {other_settlement} has a woman who paints small pictures on flat stones and leaves them along the paths.
details: He said travelers find them like little surprises. A bird on one, a leaf on another, a sunset on the next.
She doesn't sign them. People only know it's her because someone saw her painting at dawn.
reactions: Anonymous kindness is the purest kind. She's decorating the world without wanting credit.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a well rope snap while someone was halfway through pulling up a full bucket. Water everywhere.
details: A traveler said the bucket hit the water so hard it splashed up and soaked the person standing at the well.
They replaced the rope the same day. Some lessons you only need once.
reactions: Ropes wear out quietly. The snap is how they tell you it's too late.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a cat that sleeps in the sun on the same patch of path every afternoon. People step around it.
details: A peddler said the cat doesn't move for anyone. Carts go around, children step over, dogs give it space.
It's been claiming that spot for years. At this point it's an institution.
reactions: A cat that confident commands respect. The settlement adjusted to the cat, not the other way around.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has a surplus of wool this year. More than they can weave or trade.
details: He said they're storing bales of it and hoping the price holds until they can move it.
Good problem to have, but storage costs something too. Nothing stays free for long.
reactions: Surplus sounds good until you're paying to store it. Timing the trade is the real skill.

statCheck.pass: Wool stored improperly attracts moths. If they're not using cedar or lavender in the bales, they'll have holes before they have buyers.
statCheck.fail: I don't know wool. I just wear it.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a pair of brothers who compete over everything. Who finishes work first, who carries more, who eats faster.
details: A trader said the competition has been going since childhood. Neither keeps score but both know the tally.
It makes them both work harder, so nobody complains. The settlement benefits.
reactions: Rivalry that produces good work is rivalry worth keeping. As long as nobody gets bitter.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} found a natural spring behind their settlement that nobody knew about. Clean water, no digging needed.
details: A carter said a child stumbled on it while playing. Just bubbling out of the rock.
They've channeled it into a trough and it's already eased the pressure on the well.
reactions: Sometimes the best discoveries are accidents. The child probably got a treat for that one.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has mosquitoes in spring so bad that people wear cloth over their faces.
details: He said it's only for a few weeks, but during that stretch, outdoor work slows to a crawl.
The children have invented a game of counting bites. The record is apparently forty-seven in a day.
reactions: There's always a season you just endure. Theirs comes with welts.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has the best morning light of any place around. Warm, golden, hits the buildings just right.
details: A merchant said the whole settlement glows for about an hour after sunrise. Something about the angle of the valley.
People there don't notice it anymore. It took an outsider to point out how beautiful it is.
reactions: You stop seeing what you see every day. That's the cruelest trick of familiarity.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a woman who never throws anything away. Her house is stacked floor to ceiling.
details: A traveler said you can barely move through the place. Fabric scraps, jars, broken handles, everything saved.
She insists she'll need it all someday. Her neighbors politely disagree.
reactions: Thrift becomes hoarding so gradually that the person never notices the line they crossed.

entries.intro: A trader told me {other_settlement} settles disagreements by having both sides plant a row of something. Whoever's row grows better wins the argument.
details: He said it started as a joke and became policy. The settlement has extra rows of vegetables because of it.
Nobody argues as much anymore. It's hard to stay angry when you're weeding your debate row.
reactions: That's the most productive dispute resolution I've ever heard of.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had their grain stores invaded by mice that somehow bypassed every precaution.
details: A carter said they had tin sheets on the walls and a cat on patrol. The mice found a gap in the floor.
By the time they noticed, the damage was done. A tenth of the stores, gone.
reactions: Mice find the one weakness you didn't think of. Every single time.

statCheck.pass: Mice can squeeze through gaps smaller than a finger. If they sealed the walls but not the floor joints, the floor is where they came in.
statCheck.fail: Mice get in. That's what they do. I don't know the engineering of it.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a man who walks the perimeter of the settlement every evening before bed. Same route, same time.
details: A peddler said it's not a guard duty. He's just checking that everything looks right. Fences, gates, animals.
He's been doing it for years. The settlement sleeps better knowing someone looked.
reactions: Quiet vigilance is its own kind of care. The people who check the locks deserve more credit.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} has been getting dust storms that coat everything in fine grit. Teeth, food, clothes, all of it.
details: He said people there cover their faces and keep working through it. They're used to it.
Visitors cough for days afterward. The locals just spit and carry on.
reactions: You can get used to anything if it happens often enough. Doesn't mean you should have to.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a rule that whoever finishes work first that day lights the communal fire. Small honor, but people race for it.
details: A trader said the fire gets lit earlier and earlier as people rush their tasks. Quality has dipped slightly.
The elder might need to rethink the tradition before someone builds a fence with three nails.
reactions: Incentives shape behavior. Even small ones. Especially small ones.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a pair of ducks that follow the baker around every morning. Waddle right behind, single file.
details: A peddler said the baker feeds them scraps and now they won't leave. Dawn parade, every day.
Children line up to watch. It's become the settlement's best entertainment.
reactions: Feed an animal twice and you have a pet. Feed it three times and you have a follower.

entries.intro: A carter mentioned that {other_settlement} can't agree on the color to paint their meeting hall. It's been bare wood for a year.
details: He said four factions have formed. Red, blue, green, and leave-it-as-is. Meetings about it go nowhere.
The building gets more weathered every month they argue. The wood is making the decision for them.
reactions: Democracy at its finest. Argue about the color until the building rots.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has an apple tree that produces fruit twice a year. Nobody's seen one like it before.
details: A merchant said the spring crop is small and tart, the autumn crop is big and sweet. Same tree, different fruit.
People come from neighboring settlements just to see it. The owner is tired of visitors.
reactions: Nature does what it wants. Sometimes what it wants is surprising.

statCheck.pass: Some apple varieties bloom twice when the climate is mild enough. It's rare but not impossible. The tree found its perfect spot.
statCheck.fail: I don't know trees well enough to explain it. Strange things grow.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a problem with their communal saw. It's dull and nobody wants to spend the time sharpening it.
details: A trader said every job takes twice as long because the saw drags instead of cuts.
People blame each other for dulling it and nobody steps up. Classic communal property problem.
reactions: When something belongs to everyone, it belongs to nobody. And nobody sharpens it.

entries.intro: A traveler told me {other_settlement} smells like pine all year round. The trees around them shed needles into everything.
details: He said even the bread tastes faintly of pine. The resin gets into the firewood and flavors the smoke.
People there don't notice it. Visitors either love it or can't stop sneezing.
reactions: Pine is a clean smell, at least. Better than some settlements I've heard about.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had someone accidentally drop a hammer down the well. It's still there.
details: A peddler said they tried hooking it out with a rope and a bent nail. No luck.
The water still tastes fine. The hammer's just living there now.
reactions: Some losses you accept and move on from. The well has a hammer. That's just how it is.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a grandmother who insists on feeding every visitor until they can't move. Refuses to take no for an answer.
details: A merchant said he tried to leave after two helpings and she blocked the door with a third bowl.
The settlement finds it hilarious. The visitors find it overwhelming.
reactions: Aggressive hospitality is still hospitality. You leave full, which is more than most places offer.

entries.intro: A peddler told me {other_settlement} has a creek that turns milky white for a few days every spring. Sediment from upstream.
details: He said the first time it happened, people panicked. Now they just wait it out.
It clears on its own within a week. They use the well water until then.
reactions: Nature's rhythms stop being alarming once you've seen them repeat. The first time is always the scariest.

statCheck.pass: Glacial melt upstream carries fine rock dust. The white color is ground stone suspended in the water. Harmless, just looks unsettling.
statCheck.fail: White water is odd. I'd wait for it to clear too.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} tried to train a crow to carry messages. The crow learned to fly away and not come back.
details: A trader said they spent weeks feeding it, naming it, teaching it routes. First real flight. Gone.
The trainer insists the next one will work. Nobody shares the optimism.
reactions: You can't train wildness out of a wild bird. The crow knew exactly what it was doing.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a stretch of ground near their settlement that hums after sunset. Low vibration, like something underground.
details: A carter said the locals think it's water moving through stone. Visitors find it unsettling.
It's been happening as long as anyone remembers. Part of the landscape now.
reactions: The ground does things we don't always understand. Doesn't mean it's doing anything dangerous.

statCheck.pass: Underground water flowing through narrow stone channels creates resonance. The stone amplifies the vibration. It's common near limestone beds.
statCheck.fail: Ground noises are ground noises. I'm not a geologist.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} had a big argument about whether to name a stray dog that showed up. Naming it means keeping it.
details: He said half the settlement was already calling it something before the debate was over.
The dog didn't wait for consensus. It moved into the warmest spot it could find and stayed.
reactions: The dog decided before the people did. That's usually how it goes with strays.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has the steepest roofs anyone's seen. Snow slides right off, but rain sounds like a drum.
details: A peddler said the noise during storms is incredible. Like living inside a drum.
They built them that way on purpose after one winter buried their old roofs under snow.
reactions: Every solution creates a new problem. But dry floors beat quiet ones.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a man who's kept a daily tally of the weather for twenty years. Sunshine, rain, wind, everything marked on a wall.
details: A traveler said the wall is covered in small marks and symbols. Only the man himself can read it.
He uses it to predict planting dates. His predictions are better than guessing, worse than perfect.
reactions: Twenty years of patience. Even if the system is imperfect, the commitment is impressive.

statCheck.pass: Long-term weather records are invaluable for farming. Patterns over twenty years reveal cycles you'd never notice year to year.
statCheck.fail: Keeping track of weather sounds tedious. But some people like that sort of thing.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said they've been dealing with a well that drops in level every summer. Getting worse each year.
details: He said by late summer they're rationing water. People line up before dawn to fill buckets.
They've talked about digging deeper, but nobody wants to pay for it when the water comes back in autumn.
reactions: A problem that fixes itself every year is a problem people never solve. Until the year it doesn't fix itself.

statCheck.pass: A well that drops seasonally is tapping a shallow aquifer. They need to go deeper to hit the year-round water table, or they'll be rationing every summer.
statCheck.fail: Wells go up and down. I don't know the science of it.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} made a batch of cheese so good that a trader bought the entire supply. Left nothing for the locals.
details: A peddler said people were furious. Their best cheese, sold out from under them in a single morning.
The cheesemaker felt terrible. Made a double batch next month and saved half for the settlement.
reactions: Success has a way of creating new problems. Nobody expects their cheese to be too popular.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} lost power over who tends the communal garden. Three people all claiming they're in charge.
details: A merchant said the garden has three different planting schemes now. Half the rows contradict each other.
The vegetables don't care about the politics. They're growing fine despite the chaos.
reactions: Plants don't need leadership. People do. The garden will outlast the argument.

entries.intro: A carter said {other_settlement} has a woman who makes the strongest vinegar anyone's tasted. One drop and your eyes water.
details: He said she uses overripe fruit and lets it ferment longer than anyone else dares.
Traders buy it for preserving, not for eating. A little goes a very long way.
reactions: Good vinegar is underrated. It preserves food, cleans wounds, and keeps you honest about your cooking.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a cobblestone path so uneven that people twist their ankles on it regularly.
details: A trader said the stones were laid by someone who was enthusiastic but not skilled. Gaps and bumps everywhere.
They'd re-lay it, but that means admitting the first attempt was a failure. Pride gets in the way.
reactions: A bad path you're too proud to fix is worse than a dirt track. At least dirt is honest about what it is.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} hosted a group of traveling tinkers for a week. Every pot and pan in the settlement got mended.
details: A peddler said the tinkers worked fast and cheap. People were pulling out things that had been broken for years.
When they left, the settlement realized how much they'd been working around broken equipment.
reactions: You get used to what's broken. Takes an outsider fixing it to remind you how things should work.

entries.intro: A traveler told me {other_settlement} has the loudest crickets he's ever heard. Summer nights are a wall of sound.
details: He said you can't hold a conversation outdoors after sunset. You'd have to shout.
People there keep their shutters closed at night. It's the only way to sleep.
reactions: Every place has its noise. Theirs just comes from insects instead of neighbors.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a tradition of planting a tree for every child born there. A grove of birth-trees.
details: A merchant said the oldest trees are massive. The youngest are saplings barely knee-high.
People visit their own tree on birthdays. Some talk to them.
reactions: Growing alongside a tree is a beautiful idea. Roots and all.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a batch of dye go wrong and stain half the town's clothing a blotchy green. Still wearing it.
details: A peddler said people tried to wash it out and it set deeper. Now it's permanent.
They've decided to call it a local style. Confidence is half of fashion, apparently.
reactions: When the mistake is too big to fix, you own it. That's the only move.

entries.intro: A merchant told me the elder of {other_settlement} has a policy of welcoming every trader with a warm drink. Before any business.
details: He said it changes the tone of every transaction. You feel like a guest, not a transaction.
It probably costs them a few cups of tea a week. The goodwill it buys is worth ten times that.
reactions: A small gesture of hospitality pays for itself in loyalty. Smart, whether they mean it or not.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has had three straight years of carpenter ants in their support beams. The structural wood is getting soft.
details: A carter said you can push a finger into some of the beams. Not a comforting test.
Replacing the beams means taking buildings apart. Nobody wants to start that project.
reactions: Putting off structural repairs is borrowing time from the future. And the future charges interest.

statCheck.pass: Carpenter ants follow moisture. If the beams are damp, fixing the roof drainage would starve the ants out. The ants are a symptom, not the cause.
statCheck.fail: Wood rot is bad. I know that much.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a natural hot spring close enough to use in winter. People soak in it after work.
details: A traveler said the steam rises off the water and the whole area smells faintly of sulfur.
The warmth after a cold day is apparently worth the smell. Nobody complains.
reactions: Hot water in winter is a luxury most settlements would trade dearly for.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has a boy who draws faces in the dirt with a stick. Portraits of everyone in the settlement.
details: He said the likenesses are impressive for someone so young. You can tell who's who.
The boy draws them and the wind erases them. He doesn't seem to mind. He just draws them again.
reactions: Talent that young either grows into something remarkable or gets buried by life. I hope it's the first.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} tried to start a weekly rest day. It lasted two weeks before everyone got restless.
details: A trader said people don't know what to do when they're told not to work. They fidget.
By the third week, everyone was back to working seven days. The idea died quietly.
reactions: Rest is a skill. If you've never practiced it, you can't just start on command.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a visiting merchant who overcharged them for salt and they didn't realize until he was long gone.
details: A carter said the price was triple what anyone here pays. They'd never bought from an outsider before.
Now they compare prices before buying. The expensive salt was a cheap lesson, all things considered.
reactions: Getting cheated once makes you careful forever. That's the silver lining of being taken advantage of.

statCheck.pass: Isolated settlements without trade experience are easy marks. The fix isn't suspicion, it's knowledge. Learn what things cost before someone tells you.
statCheck.fail: Salt's expensive. Or it isn't. I don't follow prices.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} has a sunset that paints the clouds pink and orange for half an hour every clear evening.
details: He said he sat and watched the whole thing. Couldn't help it. The light changes every minute.
The locals barely glance at it. They've seen a thousand of them.
reactions: Seeing beauty every day doesn't make it less beautiful. Just less noticed.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a fence gate that everyone latches differently. Some left, some right, some just lean a stick against it.
details: A peddler said the gate is technically closed, always, but how it got closed changes by the hour.
Nobody's agreed on a method and at this point nobody will. The gate works. That's enough.
reactions: Agreement on small things is harder than agreement on big things. People die on the strangest hills.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a spring celebration where everyone contributes one dish and they eat until nothing's left. Takes all day.
details: A trader said the variety is staggering. Every household brings something different.
By evening, people are lying in the grass too full to move. The children are the last ones standing.
reactions: A community that eats together stays together. Especially when the food is good.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said their main well has been tasting chalky lately. Nobody knows why.
details: He said people have been walking further to use the backup spring. Adds half an hour to the morning.
The taste isn't dangerous, just unpleasant. They keep hoping it'll clear on its own.
reactions: Water you can't trust changes the whole rhythm of a settlement.

statCheck.pass: Chalky water usually means the well hit a limestone pocket. Harmless, but the taste won't go away without lining the shaft.
statCheck.fail: Water's water. As long as it doesn't make you sick, I wouldn't worry.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a woman who can identify any plant by smell alone. Doesn't even need to look at it.
details: A peddler said she once named a dried herb from three paces away. Got it right, too.
People bring her bundles of forage to sort. She charges nothing, just enjoys being useful.
reactions: A nose like that is worth more than most tools. You can't buy that kind of knowledge.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had their communal grain mill break down right at harvest. Worst possible timing.
details: A carter said they spent three days grinding by hand while someone repaired the mechanism.
Arms were sore across the whole settlement. People were sharing muscle rubs.
reactions: You don't appreciate a mill until you have to do its job yourself.

statCheck.pass: Mills break when the bearings wear. If they'd greased the axle more often, it would have lasted through harvest.
statCheck.fail: Machines break sometimes. That's all I know.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has the friendliest dogs he's ever seen. Every one of them walks up wagging.
details: He said he couldn't take ten steps without a dog nudging his hand for a scratch.
The dogs are well-fed and calm. Says a lot about how the settlement treats its animals.
reactions: Happy dogs mean happy people. I've never known that rule to fail.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has been arguing about whether to build a second well or deepen the first. It's been going on for months.
details: A traveler said both sides have good points and neither will budge. Classic standoff.
Meanwhile the original well does fine. The argument is about the future, not the present.
reactions: Planning for problems you don't have yet is either wise or a waste of breath. Depends on the day.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a man who won't eat anything he didn't grow or catch himself. Stubborn about it.
details: A peddler said the man's been that way for years. Won't touch bread from anyone else's oven.
His garden is immaculate. Whatever his reasons, the results speak for themselves.
reactions: Self-reliance taken that far is either admirable or exhausting. Probably both.

entries.intro: A carter mentioned {other_settlement} has a problem with smoke from their fire pit drifting into people's homes. Wind comes from the wrong direction.
details: He said they've tried building walls around the pit, but the wind just finds another path.
People close their shutters when the wind shifts. It's become habit.
reactions: Living with the wind is a negotiation you never win. You just learn to adapt.

statCheck.pass: Wind follows the path of least resistance. If they built the wall on the upwind side instead of around the pit, it would redirect the smoke away from the buildings.
statCheck.fail: Smoke goes where it goes. I'm not clever enough to fix wind.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} found a clay deposit near their settlement. Good quality, smooth and workable.
details: A trader said someone there already started making bowls and cups. The first batch cracked, the second held.
They're talking about trading the pottery if they can make enough. Early days, though.
reactions: New resources change a settlement's prospects. Even something as simple as clay.

statCheck.pass: Good clay is about the mineral content. Too much sand and it crumbles, too much calcium and it won't fire evenly. Smooth clay like that fires well.
statCheck.fail: Clay is clay to me. I couldn't tell good from bad.

entries.intro: I heard the road between here and {other_settlement} gets muddy enough in spring to swallow a cart wheel.
details: A merchant said he lost half a day digging his cart out. Had to unload and reload everything.
People there know to wait until the road dries, but outsiders learn the hard way.
reactions: A bad road costs you time or goods. Sometimes both.

statCheck.pass: That stretch sits in a low spot where water collects. Gravel or crushed stone would raise the road bed and let it drain, but it's expensive.
statCheck.fail: Mud is mud. I avoid that road when I can.

entries.intro: A peddler told me {other_settlement} has a couple that's been married for forty years and still bickers over how to stack firewood.
details: He said the wife insists on bark side up and the husband stacks bark side down. Every single time.
Neither budges. They've built separate stacks for decades.
reactions: Forty years of disagreement on firewood and they're still together. That's its own kind of love.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} produces the driest, crumbliest bread anyone's ever tried to eat. Falls apart in your hands.
details: A trader said the baker there doesn't believe in adding enough water. Thinks it makes the bread soft and weak.
People soak it in soup or broth before eating. It's the only way to get through a slice.
reactions: Bad bread is a personal offense to me. Life's too short for crumbly bread.

statCheck.pass: The baker's probably using old flour that's lost its moisture. Fresh-ground grain holds water better and makes a softer loaf.
statCheck.fail: I eat bread, I don't bake it. Can't tell you what's wrong.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a young girl who can hold her breath longer than any adult there. Won every contest they've put together.
details: A carter said she just sits calmly while the adults turn red and give up. Doesn't even look strained.
The adults have stopped competing. It's embarrassing to lose to a child.
reactions: Children have a way of humbling adults. Usually without meaning to.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said their nights have been getting louder. Frogs in a pond nearby, all croaking at once.
details: He said it starts at dusk and doesn't stop until dawn. A wall of noise.
Some people find it soothing. Others stuff cloth in their ears.
reactions: Frogs mean clean water, at least. A loud pond is a healthy pond.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a tradition where new arrivals dig one shovelful from the community garden. Symbolic welcome.
details: A peddler said it sounds small, but people remember that first dig. Makes them feel like they belong.
The garden's been dug and replanted so many times the soil is the richest in the settlement.
reactions: Rituals that make people feel included are worth keeping, even if they're simple.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a fence blow down in a storm and nobody's rebuilt it because they can't agree on where it should go.
details: A trader said three neighbors all claim the fence was on their boundary. Nobody can prove it.
The gap in the fence has been there for two seasons now. Animals wander freely.
reactions: Boundary disputes outlast fences. The wind just made it everyone's problem.

statCheck.pass: Boundary disputes like that usually have a solution. Measure equal distances from both houses and split the difference. Pride is what keeps them from trying.
statCheck.fail: Property lines aren't my area. I'd stay out of it.

entries.intro: A traveler said {other_settlement} has the clearest night sky he's ever seen. Stars thick enough to cast shadows.
details: He said there are no large fires at night, so the dark is deep and the sky is bright.
People there take the sky for granted. The traveler sat outside for an hour just looking up.
reactions: A clear sky is free and beautiful, and most people are asleep when it's at its best.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a problem with crows getting into their drying racks. Meat and fruit both.
details: A carter said they've tried scarecrows, bells, cloth covers. The crows figure it out within a day.
One woman moved her rack indoors. The rest are still fighting the birds.
reactions: Crows are smarter than most people give them credit for. You don't outwit a crow, you just outlast one.

statCheck.pass: Crows learn by watching. If one crow solves the deterrent, the rest copy it. You need to change your approach every few days to stay ahead.
statCheck.fail: I've never had to fight crows for my food. Can't help you there.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} put in a communal herb garden this year. Rosemary, thyme, sage, the basics.
details: A merchant said anyone can pick what they need. No one person tends it, but it stays healthy.
The cooking in the settlement has improved noticeably. Small investment, big returns.
reactions: Herbs cost almost nothing and improve everything. Surprised more settlements don't do this.

entries.intro: A carter said the elder in {other_settlement} has a habit of talking to himself when he thinks nobody's around. Full conversations.
details: He said people hear both sides of an argument coming from behind closed doors. One voice, two opinions.
Nobody minds, since his decisions tend to be sound. Whatever his process is, it works.
reactions: Thinking out loud is just thinking with witnesses. As long as the answers are good, who cares.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} lost a goat that somehow ended up on somebody's roof. Nobody can figure out how it got up there.
details: A peddler said the goat seemed perfectly content. Ate the thatch for half a day before they coaxed it down.
The roof now has a bald patch. The goat has a full belly and no regrets.
reactions: Goats go where goats want to go. Fences, walls, roofs. None of it matters to a goat.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} traded a cart of {resource_type} for a cart of salt last season. Both sides felt cheated afterward.
details: A trader said the salt merchants thought they overpaid and {other_settlement} thought the same about the {resource_type}.
Despite the grumbling, they'll probably trade again next year. That's how it goes.
reactions: A trade where both sides feel cheated is either the worst deal or the fairest. Hard to tell.

entries.intro: A traveler told me {other_settlement} has a man who hums the same tune every morning while he works. Nobody knows where the tune comes from.
details: He said the man's been humming it for years. Asked about it once and the man couldn't explain it.
Some of the children have picked it up. The tune is spreading through the settlement.
reactions: Music gets into people whether they mean it to or not. That tune belongs to the whole settlement now.

statCheck.pass: Tunes like that often come from childhood. He probably heard it from a parent and it stuck so deep he can't remember learning it.
statCheck.fail: Music sticks in the head. I don't know why some tunes more than others.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a chicken that lays eggs with shells so thick you need a knife to crack them.
details: A merchant said the eggs taste the same as any other. It's just the shell that's remarkable.
People there have given up cracking them on bowls. Straight to a blade every time.
reactions: Strange birds are more common than people think. Nature experiments constantly.

statCheck.pass: Thick shells usually mean the bird has more calcium than normal in its diet. If they're feeding it ground shells or chalk, that would do it.
statCheck.fail: Eggs are eggs. I don't know what makes the shells different.

entries.intro: I heard a family in {other_settlement} accidentally planted twice as much as they needed this year. Drowning in turnips.
details: A peddler said they miscounted the rows and didn't notice until everything came up.
They've been giving turnips to anyone who'll take them. Even the goats are tired of turnips.
reactions: Too much of anything becomes a burden. Even food.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has a boy who's afraid of chickens. Screams and runs every time one comes near.
details: He said the chickens seem to know it and follow the boy deliberately. Birds have a sense for weakness.
The adults try not to laugh. They mostly succeed.
reactions: Fear doesn't have to make sense. That boy's terror is real to him, and the chickens aren't helping.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a roof cave in under the weight of wet snow. Nobody hurt, just a mess.
details: A carter said they'd been told to clear the snow earlier and didn't listen.
The cleanup took longer than the repair. Everything inside was soaked.
reactions: Wet snow is heavier than people think. One day of neglect can cost you a roof.

statCheck.pass: Wet snow can weigh ten times what dry snow does, and a roof built for dry winters fails when the climate shifts wetter. They need steeper pitch or regular clearing.
statCheck.fail: Snow is heavy. I know that much.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a woman who can sharpen any blade to a mirror edge. People bring her their worst tools.
details: A trader said she uses a stone she found years ago that nobody else can get results from.
The blades she works on stay sharp twice as long as normal. People swear by her.
reactions: Good sharpening is an art, and most people who think they can do it are wrong.

statCheck.pass: The stone matters more than the hand. Certain river stones have the right grit to hone a fine edge. She found a good one and learned how to use it.
statCheck.fail: I sharpen my own tools and they're adequate. That's the best I can say.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has a well-worn path between two buildings where nothing grows. People have walked it bare.
details: He said the dirt is packed so hard it's like stone. Rain runs off it instead of soaking in.
Nobody planned the path. A thousand trips back and forth built it over years.
reactions: The paths people actually use are never the ones you planned.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a child climb the tallest tree there and refuse to come down. Stayed up for half a day.
details: A merchant said the parents tried bargaining, threatening, and ignoring. Nothing worked.
The child came down when they got hungry. Problem solved itself, as children's problems often do.
reactions: Stubbornness in children is annoying now and useful later. If they survive childhood.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has been trading with a settlement I've never even heard of. Somewhere far south.
details: A carter said the goods were different from anything around here. Different cloth, different tools.
Nobody knows much about the place. The trader who brought the goods wasn't talkative.
reactions: There's always somewhere further away that you've never heard of. The world is bigger than we think.

statCheck.pass: Far-traveled goods tend to carry a markup. If {other_settlement} is paying premium prices for exotic tools, they might not be getting the best deal.
statCheck.fail: I don't know enough about southern trade to have an opinion.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they've had a great year for berries. Bushes loaded, more than they can pick.
details: He said they're preserving what they can and the rest is just falling and rotting on the ground.
Birds are fat and happy over there. Every branch has a bird on it.
reactions: A good berry year lifts everyone's spirits. Sweet things make hard work easier to bear.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a dog that herds the chickens back to the coop every evening without being told.
details: A traveler said the dog just started doing it one day. No training, no encouragement.
The chickens seem resigned to it. They file into the coop in a neat little line.
reactions: A dog that gives itself a job is the best kind of dog.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a cooking fire get out of hand and scorch the side of a building. Just the outside, nothing serious.
details: A peddler said they put it out fast, but the scorch mark is still visible.
Nobody claims responsibility. The fire pit is now three paces further from the building.
reactions: Fire teaches its lessons fast. A scorch mark is a cheap reminder.

statCheck.pass: Cooking fires near buildings need a stone or dirt buffer, three paces minimum. One stray spark is all it takes, and they're lucky the lesson was cheap.
statCheck.fail: Fire is dangerous near buildings. Everyone knows that.

entries.intro: A carter told me {other_settlement} has started putting aside a portion of every harvest for lean times. Communal reserve.
details: He said people grumbled at first, but last winter's shortage convinced the holdouts.
It's not much. A tenth of the yield. But a tenth adds up over a good year.
reactions: Saving when times are good is the hardest kind of discipline. Easy to spend, hard to store.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a spot where the ground stays warm even in winter. Animals sleep there.
details: A trader said dogs and cats pile up on it when the cold hits. Visitors think it's strange.
Locals say it's been that way as long as anyone remembers. They don't question it.
reactions: The ground has its own rules. Warm spots, cold spots. You learn where things are and live with it.

statCheck.pass: Geothermal heat from deep rock can warm the surface in small areas. Nothing alarming. It just means there's hot stone far below.
statCheck.fail: Warm ground in winter? Sounds odd, but animals seem to know what's safe.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a man who snores so loud his neighbors can hear it through the walls. Every night.
details: A peddler said the man sleeps like the dead while the rest of the house lies awake.
They've tried stuffing cloth in his mouth, rolling him over, everything. Nothing works.
reactions: A snorer doesn't suffer, but everyone else does.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} sends their children out to gather kindling every morning before breakfast. Part of growing up there.
details: He said the children race each other to fill their bundles first. Makes a chore into a game.
By the time they sit down to eat, the fire's stocked for the day.
reactions: Teaching children to contribute early is a kindness disguised as work.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has been getting complaints about a rooster that crows two hours before dawn. Every single morning.
details: A carter said the owner insists the rooster is fine and everyone else's sleep schedule is wrong.
People have started going to bed earlier just to compensate. The rooster is winning.
reactions: One rooster can change the rhythm of a whole settlement. That's power, in its own small way.

statCheck.pass: Roosters crow based on internal rhythm, not the sun, so a rooster that crows early won't change. The only fix is moving the bird further from the sleeping quarters.
statCheck.fail: Roosters crow when they want. I don't know why.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a traveling musician pass through and play for a whole evening. People still talk about it.
details: A trader said the musician played songs nobody had heard before. Simple tunes, but they stuck.
People there have been humming the melodies for weeks. Can't get them out of their heads.
reactions: Good music lingers long after the musician leaves. That's the mark of real skill.

entries.intro: A peddler told me {other_settlement} has a woman who keeps bees. Says she talks to them and they don't sting her.
details: He said she walks right up to the hives bare-handed. The bees crawl on her and she doesn't flinch.
Her honey is the best around. Whatever she's doing, the bees approve.
reactions: Some people understand animals in a way the rest of us never will.

statCheck.pass: Calm movement and no fear scent. Bees sting when they sense a threat. If she's genuinely relaxed, the bees have no reason to attack.
statCheck.fail: Bees sting me. That's all I know about bees.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a tree stump in the middle of their common area that nobody will remove. Too many roots.
details: A merchant said they've hacked at the roots for years and given up. The stump outlasted three axes.
People sit on it now. It's become a bench by default.
