details: A trader said one house gets morning sun and the other gets evening shade. Different preferences.
Nobody thought to suggest a swap until someone said it as a joke. Turns out the joke was the answer.
reactions: Problems that seem stuck sometimes have obvious solutions. You just need fresh eyes.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has had their best year for {resource_type} in a long time. Everything lined up. Weather, timing, effort.
details: A merchant said the whole settlement's mood is better when production is up. People smile more.
A good year makes last year's struggles feel worth enduring.
reactions: Prosperity doesn't solve everything, but it makes all the other problems smaller.

entries.intro: A carter told me {other_settlement} has a man who snores so loud his neighbors can hear him through the walls. Every single night.
details: He said the man's wife sleeps fine, somehow. Everyone else lies awake.
People have tried asking him to sleep on his side. He rolls back within minutes.
reactions: One person's snoring becoming a settlement-wide problem is both funny and miserable.

statCheck.pass: Elevating the head with an extra blanket roll opens the airway and reduces snoring. Most people don't try it because they don't realize the angle matters.
statCheck.fail: Snoring is a nuisance I have no cure for.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} built a bench near the road for travelers to rest. Simple. Just a plank on two stumps.
details: A peddler said it's the first thing you see coming in. A small gesture that says you're welcome.
Traders stop there to rest their legs. A few minutes' rest changes your impression of a place.
reactions: Hospitality starts with small things. A bench says more than a speech.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a dispute over who owns a particular apple tree. It sits right on the boundary between two plots.
details: A trader said both families pick from it and both claim the other is taking more than their share.
The tree produces plenty for both. The argument is about principle, not apples.
reactions: Boundary disputes aren't really about the thing on the boundary. They're about respect.

statCheck.pass: The roots of an apple tree spread wider than its canopy. Whoever's soil feeds the roots has the stronger claim, but good luck explaining that to two angry families.
statCheck.fail: Tree ownership disputes are beyond me. I just eat the apples.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} has a woman who mends clothes so well you can't find the seam. Invisible repairs.
details: He said she matches thread color and stitch tension so precisely that the mend disappears into the fabric.
People send clothes from other settlements to her. The reputation is deserved.
reactions: Making something damaged look whole again is an underrated skill.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had their root cellar flood after a week of heavy rain. Most of the winter stores were ruined.
details: A carter said the water came up through the floor. By the time they noticed, the vegetables were sitting in mud.
They salvaged the top shelves, but the roots and tubers on the ground level were lost.
reactions: Losing winter stores is losing sleep for months. You spend the rest of the year catching up.

statCheck.pass: A drainage trench around the cellar's outside wall, filled with gravel, would channel groundwater away before it seeps through. Takes a day to dig and saves a season's food.
statCheck.fail: Water goes where it wants. Underground storage is always a gamble in wet years.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has someone who ties knots so well that sailors would be jealous. Every hitch holds perfectly.
details: A peddler said the man ties loads on carts and they never shift. Not once.
Traders specifically request him when they need something secured for the road.
reactions: A skill like that doesn't look impressive until you see what happens when it's absent.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said their only cat had kittens and now they've got more cats than they know what to do with.
details: He said every household has at least one. The mice are gone but the cats are everywhere.
Some people are offering kittens to traders just to reduce the numbers.
reactions: A cat surplus is a problem that solves another problem. Depends which one bothers you more.

statCheck.pass: One female cat can produce three litters a year, each with four to six kittens. Without separation, the numbers double every few months.
statCheck.fail: Cats multiply. That's about all I can contribute.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has been trading {resource_type} at lower prices than anyone expected. Undercutting the usual rates.
details: A merchant said they're flooding the market to move surplus quickly before it deteriorates.
Other settlements aren't happy about it. Low prices help buyers but hurt competing sellers.
reactions: Cheap goods make enemies among neighbors. Trade relationships are delicate things.

statCheck.pass: Selling at a loss to move perishable surplus is smart in the short term, but if it becomes a pattern, they'll depress the market and hurt their own long-term prices.
statCheck.fail: Low prices sound good to me. I'm not a merchant, though.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has an old man who remembers the exact weather on the day each of his grandchildren was born. Rain, sun, wind, all of it.
details: A carter said the man recites it like a poem. Born on a cold morning with frost on the ground, or born under a warm evening sky.
The grandchildren love hearing it. The weather becomes part of who they are.
reactions: Memory is a gift when it's used to make people feel connected to the world they arrived in.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has a creek that floods every spring and deposits rich mud across the nearby fields. Annoying but useful.
details: He said the mud is dark and thick. Crops planted in it grow taller than anything else.
People complain about the flooding every year and then plant in the mud every year. The cycle continues.
reactions: Nature's gifts rarely come without inconvenience attached.

statCheck.pass: Flood deposits are rich because the water carries nutrients from upstream soil. The same process that makes river deltas fertile makes their fields productive.
statCheck.fail: Rich mud grows good crops. I know that much, but not why.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a carpenter who measures everything three times and still cuts wrong occasionally. Meticulous but unlucky.
details: A trader said the man's work is solid when the measurements cooperate. Most of the time it's fine.
The scraps pile from his mis-cuts is the biggest in the settlement. He uses them for kindling.
reactions: Precision and accuracy aren't the same thing. You can be careful and still be off.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a season where the berries came in early and everyone gorged themselves. Then the birds came for what was left.
details: A merchant said the bushes were stripped bare in a few days between the people and the birds.
Nobody preserved any because there was so much fresh that they thought it would last.
reactions: Abundance makes people careless. You eat like it won't end, and then it ends.

statCheck.pass: Early berries should be preserved immediately because the ripening window is shorter than usual. Drying half while eating the other half gives you stores and satisfaction both.
statCheck.fail: Berries don't last. That's a lesson most people learn the hard way.

entries.intro: A carter mentioned {other_settlement} has a family that cooks for the whole settlement once a month. Everyone brings something, one family does the cooking.
details: He said the meals are uneven. Sometimes excellent, sometimes questionable. Depends on who's cooking that month.
Nobody complains because next month it's their turn. Criticism has a way of coming back.
reactions: Shared meals build trust even when the food is bad. It's the sitting together that matters.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} found mold in their flour stores. Had to throw out three sacks.
details: A peddler said the storage area got damp after a leak nobody noticed for weeks.
Three sacks of flour is a lot to lose. That's weeks of bread gone.
reactions: Mold doesn't announce itself. By the time you smell it, the damage is done.

statCheck.pass: Flour stored in a damp environment grows mold because the moisture activates spores that are already in the grain. Keeping the air dry and the sacks off the ground is the only prevention.
statCheck.fail: Moldy flour is ruined flour. I don't know much about preventing it.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a young man who sharpens every blade in the settlement for free. Says it relaxes him.
details: A trader said the knives in that settlement are the sharpest he's ever seen. Every single one.
The man sits outside in the evenings with a whetstone and works through a pile of blades. Methodical, quiet.
reactions: A settlement full of sharp tools works better and doesn't even realize why.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has been having trouble getting traders to come through. The road's in poor condition.
details: He said the last stretch before the settlement is rutted and muddy. Carts get stuck regularly.
Traders avoid it and go to easier destinations. Fewer visitors means fewer goods.
reactions: A bad road isolates a settlement more effectively than distance does.

statCheck.pass: Gravel in the worst ruts and a drainage ditch along the edge would fix it in a week of labor. The problem isn't knowledge, it's organizing the work.
statCheck.fail: Bad roads keep people away. That's about all I can say.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a tradition of leaving a lantern burning outside the meeting hall on nights when the weather's bad. A signal that travelers are welcome.
details: A peddler said he saw the lantern through the rain once and knew exactly what it meant. Warmth and shelter ahead.
The oil costs them, but the goodwill it earns is worth more.
reactions: A single light in bad weather can change a traveler's whole night.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a problem with their chimney smoking when the wind comes from the north. Half the year it's fine, half it isn't.
details: A carter said the wind pushes smoke back down the chimney and fills the room.
They've tried blocking the wind with boards. The boards blow down.
reactions: Fighting the wind with boards is fighting the wind. The wind always wins.

statCheck.pass: A rotating chimney cap that turns with the wind would solve it. The cap blocks wind from entering while still letting smoke out the leeward side.
statCheck.fail: Wind and smoke don't cooperate. I don't have a fix for that.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} holds a footrace for the children every harvest. The winner gets first pick of the pies.
details: He said the race is short but fiercely contested. Children train for it weeks in advance.
The pies are all good, so winning is about pride more than pastry.
reactions: Traditions that give children something to work toward are worth keeping.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a fence post rot through and the whole pen came down. Livestock wandered off for half a day.
details: A peddler said people spent the afternoon chasing goats and chickens in every direction.
The post looked fine on the outside. The rot was all internal.
reactions: Things that look solid can be hollow inside. True for posts, true for plans.

statCheck.pass: Posts rot at the ground line where moisture and air meet. Charring the buried end before setting it creates a barrier that slows rot by years.
statCheck.fail: Wood rots underground. I know that, but I don't know how to prevent it.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a boy who can balance on one foot longer than any adult there. Just stands there, still as a post.
details: A merchant said the boy once held it for so long that people lost interest and walked away.
He claims he thinks about nothing while he does it. Maybe that's the trick.
reactions: Pointless talents are the most entertaining kind. No pressure, just amusement.

entries.intro: A peddler told me {other_settlement} has started composting in a shared pit. The whole settlement contributes scraps.
details: He said the compost goes straight onto the gardens come spring. Soil quality has improved noticeably.
The pit smells, but nobody complains because the gardens produce more.
reactions: Waste turned into growth is the most practical kind of cooperation.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a leaking water barrel that nobody fixes because everyone assumes someone else will do it.
details: A carter said the barrel drips steadily. A puddle forms underneath every day.
People step around the puddle. Nobody stops to patch the stave.
reactions: Shared responsibility often means no responsibility. The barrel proves it.

statCheck.pass: A leaking stave just needs to swell shut. Soaking the barrel full for a day lets the wood expand and close the gap. Five minutes of effort prevents weeks of dripping.
statCheck.fail: A leaking barrel needs fixing. That's about all I can say.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a couple who always disagrees about when to plant. One says early, the other says late. Every year.
details: A trader said they compromise by planting half early and half late. The better half proves the argument.
Most years the late planting wins. The early planter hasn't conceded yet.
reactions: Stubbornness in a couple is either a disaster or a system. Depends on the couple.

statCheck.pass: Late planting avoids late frosts, which is why it usually wins. But in a warm year, early planting gives a longer growing season and higher yield. Neither strategy is always right.
statCheck.fail: When to plant is a personal decision. I go by gut feeling.

entries.intro: A merchant mentioned {other_settlement} has been seeing more traders come through this season. The word is getting out.
details: He said the settlement's reputation for fair dealing is spreading among the trading routes.
More traders means more goods and better prices. Everyone benefits.
reactions: A good reputation is the slowest thing to build and the most valuable thing to have.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has an old cart that's been patched so many times there's barely any original wood left. Ship of Theseus on wheels.
details: A peddler said every plank has been replaced at least once. The shape is the same but the material is all new.
They call it the oldest cart in the settlement, even though none of it is actually old.
reactions: At what point does a repaired thing become a new thing? Nobody agrees and nobody stops asking.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a batch of soap turn out so strong it took the color out of clothes. People's shirts went pale after one wash.
details: A carter said the lye ratio was too high. Cleaned the clothes, then cleaned the dye right off.
They've adjusted the recipe. The stained shirts are a reminder.
reactions: Getting soap wrong is one of those mistakes that touches the whole settlement at once.

statCheck.pass: Too much lye makes soap caustic. The excess alkali strips dye because dye bonds are weaker than fabric bonds. Measuring by weight instead of volume prevents the error.
statCheck.fail: Strong soap ruined clothes. That's unfortunate but beyond my knowledge.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said they've built a shared tool chest. Anyone can borrow, as long as they return it clean.
details: He said the system works because everyone knows who borrowed what. Small settlement, no hiding.
The tools stay in better shape than privately owned ones because nobody wants to be the person who broke a shared saw.
reactions: Shared tools work when everybody feels watched. Accountability is the glue.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a visitor who stayed three days and left without paying for food or lodging. Just vanished at dawn.
details: A peddler said the person was polite, friendly, ate well, and then disappeared.
People feel foolish about it. Hospitality only works when both sides respect it.
reactions: One dishonest visitor makes the next honest one answer for it. That's the cost of being cheated.

statCheck.pass: Settlements that host travelers should ask for a token deposit on arrival. Not much. Just enough that leaving without settling the bill costs something.
statCheck.fail: People take advantage sometimes. Not much you can do after the fact.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a woman who can identify any wood by smell alone. Doesn't even need to see it.
details: A merchant said she'll sniff a plank and tell you the tree, how old it was, and whether it was seasoned properly.
The woodworkers consult her before buying lumber from traders. Saves them from bad purchases.
reactions: A sharp nose is an underestimated tool. Most people don't even know what wood smells like.

entries.intro: A carter told me {other_settlement} planted a row of trees along their main path for shade. In five years they'll have a canopy.
details: He said the saplings are thin now, barely above head height. But the vision is clear.
People water them faithfully. The shade is for the next generation as much as for themselves.
reactions: Planting trees you won't sit under is the most generous thing a settlement can do.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a feud between two households over a crowing rooster. One family loves it, the other can't stand it.
details: A trader said the rooster's owner says it's a natural alarm. The neighbor says it's a natural nuisance.
They've stopped speaking to each other over a bird. The rooster, of course, doesn't care.
reactions: Noise disputes between neighbors have ended more friendships than anything else I can think of.

statCheck.pass: Roosters crow at first light because they react to the change in brightness. A darkened coop delays the crowing. Simple fix, but both sides need to want a solution.
statCheck.fail: Roosters crow. That's what they do. I don't have a solution.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a stream so clear you can count the stones on the bottom. People drink straight from it.
details: A peddler said the water tastes better than any well water he's tried. Cold and clean.
The settlement's position upstream of everything keeps the water pure.
reactions: Clean water running through your settlement is a luxury that money can't buy.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} had a whole batch of dried meat go bad because the drying shed wasn't ventilated properly.
details: He said the air was too still and moisture built up instead of escaping. The meat molded from the inside.
They've cut vents into the walls now. Lesson learned, but the lost meat stings.
reactions: Drying anything without airflow is just making a moist, dark chamber for mold.

statCheck.pass: Airflow in a drying shed needs to enter low and exit high. Warm air rises and carries moisture with it. Without that circulation, humidity stays trapped around the meat.
statCheck.fail: Meat needs air to dry. When there's no air, it rots. That much is obvious.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a man who carved a sundial into a flat rock near the well. Surprisingly accurate.
details: A carter said the shadows line up well enough to plan your day around it.
People glance at it when they draw water. It's become a natural timepiece for the whole settlement.
reactions: Marking time costs nothing when the sun does the work. Clever use of a flat rock.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has been short on candles all season. The tallow supply ran out earlier than expected.
details: A trader said evenings are dark and short there. People go to bed earlier because there's nothing to do without light.
They're rendering every scrap of fat they can find. Still not enough.
reactions: Running out of candles sounds minor until you live through a month of dark evenings.

statCheck.pass: Rush lights work as a candle substitute. Dried rushes dipped in any available fat burn for about twenty minutes each. Not ideal, but it's light when you need it.
statCheck.fail: No candles means dark evenings. I feel for them.

entries.intro: A peddler mentioned {other_settlement} has started marking their trade goods with a small stamp. A brand of origin.
details: He said the stamp is a simple design pressed into wax or burned into wood.
Traders recognize it now. The stamp is becoming a mark of reliability.
reactions: A recognizable mark builds trust faster than any amount of talking.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has someone who makes rope by hand that's stronger than anything you can buy from a traveling merchant.
details: A merchant said the rope doesn't fray or stretch. Holds knots well and lasts for years.
The man works slowly. One good rope takes days. But nobody who's used one goes back to cheaper rope.
reactions: Quality work at a slow pace beats fast work that falls apart.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a year of such mild winters that nobody cut enough firewood. Then a late cold snap hit hard.
details: A carter said the cold lasted two weeks and they burned through their small reserves in days.
People burned furniture legs and fence posts to stay warm. Desperate measures.
reactions: Mild weather makes you complacent. Winter always has one more surprise.

statCheck.pass: A late cold snap after a warm winter is actually more dangerous because stored food and fuel are already low. Keeping a reserve for the unexpected is the only insurance.
statCheck.fail: Cold weather after warm weather catches everyone off guard. Not much to say beyond that.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said they've figured out a way to keep butter from going rancid in summer. Wrapping it in wet cloth and storing it underground.
details: He said the evaporation keeps the butter cool enough to last weeks longer than usual.
The technique is simple but nobody there had thought of it until this year.
reactions: The best solutions are the ones that make you wonder why nobody tried them sooner.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a boy who climbs everything. Roofs, trees, woodpiles. Can't keep him on the ground.
details: A peddler said the boy's already fallen twice and broken nothing. Luck or skill, hard to tell.
His parents have given up stopping him. They just make sure he doesn't climb when it's wet.
reactions: Children who climb everything either become very capable or very bruised. Usually both.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a tradition where newlyweds plant a tree together. The tree grows as the marriage does.
details: A merchant said some of the older trees are tall and strong. Others died young.
Nobody talks about the dead trees, but everyone notices them.
reactions: A living symbol of a promise is beautiful and brutal. The tree doesn't lie.

entries.intro: A carter said {other_settlement} has the worst mosquitoes he's ever dealt with. Thick clouds of them near the water.
details: He said you can't stand still for more than a few seconds without getting bitten.
People there seem used to it. Visitors are the ones who suffer.
reactions: You can get used to almost anything. That doesn't mean you should have to.

statCheck.pass: Standing water breeds mosquitoes. If they drained the stagnant patches near the settlement, the population would drop within a season.
statCheck.fail: Mosquitoes are awful. I avoid places with too many of them.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a woman who keeps bees and talks to them while she works the hives. Calm voice, slow movements.
details: A trader said she never gets stung. The bees crawl on her hands like she's part of the hive.
Her honey is the best in the area. People say the bees are happier, and happy bees make better honey.
reactions: Understanding an animal's temperament is a form of skill that most people never develop.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a disagreement about whether to repair the meeting hall or build a new storage shed. They chose the shed.
details: A peddler said the meeting hall leaks but the storage shed was needed more urgently.
People meet under the leaks now and plan how to fix the roof next year. Next year keeps moving.
reactions: There's never enough labor for everything. Choosing what to fix first is its own kind of argument.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has been getting better prices for their {resource_type} since they started sorting it by quality before trading.
details: He said the top-grade material sells for nearly double what the mixed lots used to fetch.
The lower-grade stuff still sells, just separately. Sorting takes time but pays for itself.
reactions: Presenting your goods well is half the trade. Buyers pay more when they can see what they're getting.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a windstorm knock a tree onto one of their buildings. Nobody was hurt, but the roof is gone.
details: A carter said the tree had been leaning for months. Everyone noticed, nobody acted.
The building's usable except when it rains. Which is often enough to be a problem.
reactions: A leaning tree is a warning. Ignoring warnings doesn't make them go away.

statCheck.pass: Trees that lean progressively are usually losing root grip on one side due to soil erosion or rot. Taking it down before it falls is always cheaper than repairing what it lands on.
statCheck.fail: Trees fall on things sometimes. I suppose they should have cut it down.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a man who makes charcoal so clean it barely produces smoke. Potters and smiths both want it.
details: A trader said the secret is in how slowly he builds the burn pile. Days of careful stacking.
He can't produce enough to meet demand. Every batch is spoken for before it's finished.
reactions: Mastery of a simple process makes ordinary materials extraordinary.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has been dealing with a well that gives water with a funny taste. Not bad, just odd.
details: He said the taste started a few weeks ago. Faintly metallic, like licking a coin.
People still drink it because it's the only well. They just make faces while they do.
reactions: Water you can't trust the taste of is water you drink with doubt. That wears on a person.

statCheck.pass: Metallic taste in well water usually means the water table shifted and is now passing through iron-bearing rock. It's not harmful, but a filter layer of sand and charcoal at the surface would improve the taste.
statCheck.fail: Funny-tasting water would bother me too. I don't know the cause.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has an elder who settles disputes by making both parties sit in silence for an hour before speaking. Works every time.
details: A merchant said by the time the hour's up, most people have cooled down enough to talk sensibly.
Some arguments resolve themselves during the silence. People realize it wasn't worth the anger.
reactions: Silence is the cheapest tool for resolving conflict, and the one least used.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a child who draws in the dirt with a stick. Detailed pictures of animals, plants, buildings. Then people walk over them.
details: A carter said the drawings are genuinely good. Better than most adults could manage.
The child doesn't seem bothered when they're erased. Just draws more the next day.
reactions: Making something knowing it won't last takes a certain kind of peace.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} mentioned they had a fox get into the chicken coop last season. Lost four birds in one night.
details: He said the fox dug under the fence where the ground was soft. Nobody expected an approach from below.
They've buried stones along the fence line now. Expensive lesson for four chickens.
reactions: A predator only needs to succeed once. A fence needs to succeed every night.

statCheck.pass: Foxes always dig. Burying the fence a foot deep with an outward-facing apron of wire or stone makes it nearly impossible for them to tunnel under.
statCheck.fail: Foxes eat chickens. A better fence is the obvious answer.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a woman who bakes bread so good that traders detour through the settlement just to buy a loaf.
details: A peddler said the crust cracks when you break it and the inside is soft and airy. Perfect every time.
She won't share the recipe. Says it's not a recipe. It's the feel of the dough.
reactions: Bread that brings people to your door is the kind of skill that builds a settlement's reputation.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a problem with their paths turning to mud every time it rains. Ankle-deep in places.
details: A merchant said you can lose a boot in the worst spots. People step carefully or not at all.
The clay soil holds water instead of draining. Every rain makes the same mess.
reactions: Mud is a small misery that affects everything. Movement, mood, cleanliness.

statCheck.pass: Clay soil needs coarse material mixed in to drain. Gravel, crushed stone, even bark chips. Without it, water sits on the surface because clay is essentially waterproof.
statCheck.fail: Mud is mud. Some places just have it worse than others.

entries.intro: A carter said {other_settlement} has a shared garden where everyone grows what they're best at. One person does root vegetables, another does leafy greens.
details: He said the variety is better than any one person could manage alone.
They trade within the settlement. My carrots for your lettuce. Nobody goes without.
reactions: Specialization works in gardens the same way it works in everything else.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a trader deliver the wrong goods and not realize it until they were already unpacked. Confusion for everyone.
details: A peddler said the crates were mislabeled. Tools where they expected cloth, cloth where they expected tools.
The trader came back to swap them, but it took a week. A week of making do with the wrong supplies.
reactions: Mislabeled goods are a headache for everyone involved. Labels exist for a reason.

statCheck.pass: Color-coded marks on crates solve the labeling problem. A trader who moves dozens of crates can't read every label, but a red mark versus a blue mark is instant.
statCheck.fail: Wrong deliveries happen. Annoying for everyone involved.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has someone who built a loom and is weaving cloth locally for the first time. Rough but serviceable.
details: A trader said the cloth isn't as fine as what you'd get from a larger settlement, but it's there when you need it.
Not depending on traders for basic cloth changes what a settlement can survive.
reactions: Self-sufficiency in one more area means one less thing that can go wrong during a bad season.

entries.intro: A merchant mentioned {other_settlement} has a well-worn path between the settlement and the nearest stream. Packed hard from daily use.
details: He said you could follow the path in the dark just by feeling the packed earth underfoot.
Thousands of trips carrying water, one direction full, the other empty. A path worn by necessity.
reactions: The paths people make by walking them tell you everything about what matters to a settlement.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a family that keeps their goat inside the house during cold nights. Says the animal warmth helps.
details: A peddler said the goat has its own corner and seems perfectly content indoors.
Visitors find it odd. The family finds it practical.
reactions: Comfort and dignity don't always overlap. If the goat keeps the house warm, who's to argue.

statCheck.pass: A single goat produces roughly the same heat as a small fire. In a tight space, that's enough to raise the temperature noticeably. Practical, even if it smells.
statCheck.fail: A goat in the house is unusual. I suppose it works for them.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} recently traded a large amount of {resource_type} for building supplies. Investing in infrastructure.
details: A carter said the deal was favorable. They had surplus and the other party had excess timber.
Both sides walked away satisfied. That's the best kind of trade.
reactions: Trading what you have too much of for what you need is so simple it's easy to forget.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} has a problem with their food stores attracting ants. Thousands of them, in organized lines.
details: He said the ants found a crack in the wall and built a highway straight to the grain.
They've sealed every crack they can find. The ants find new ones within days.
reactions: Ants are persistent beyond reason. You can't outwork them, only outwit them.

statCheck.pass: A line of ash or crusite chalk across the entry point disrupts the scent trail ants follow. Break the trail and they lose their path. Reapply after rain.
statCheck.fail: Ants are hard to stop. I just keep my food covered and hope for the best.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has someone who can fix any broken tool. Doesn't matter what it is, they'll find a way.
details: A merchant said the person works with whatever's available. Wire, scrap metal, carved wood. Resourceful.
Throwing away a tool there is almost an insult. Everything gets a second life.
reactions: A settlement that repairs instead of replacing stretches every resource further.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has been having long arguments about nothing in particular. Boredom, mostly.
details: A peddler said people debate the best way to stack firewood like it's a matter of life and death.
When there's nothing urgent to worry about, people invent things to disagree about.
reactions: Idle arguments are actually a sign of stability. Real problems don't leave room for trivial ones.

entries.intro: A carter told me {other_settlement} found that their newest building settled unevenly. One corner sank lower than the others.
details: He said the floor slopes enough that a dropped ball rolls to one corner.
The building is usable but everything inside leans slightly. You get used to it, apparently.
reactions: A crooked building is a daily reminder that the ground beneath you has opinions.

statCheck.pass: Uneven settling means the foundation wasn't on solid ground throughout. Soft soil under one corner compresses more over time. They should have dug deeper or laid stone beneath the posts.
statCheck.fail: Buildings settle sometimes. If it's still standing, it's probably fine.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a man who plays a handmade flute in the evenings. Simple melodies, nothing showy.
details: A trader said the music drifts across the settlement as people settle in for the night.
Nobody asked him to play. He just started one evening and never stopped.
reactions: Music in the evening changes the feeling of a place. Even simple notes make a difference.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a batch of clay tiles crack in the kiln. Weeks of work ruined.
details: A merchant said the kiln got too hot too fast. Thermal shock split the tiles before they could cure evenly.
They're trying again with a slower heat-up. Learning by failing.
reactions: Craft mistakes cost time you can't get back. But the knowledge sticks.

statCheck.pass: Clay needs to heat slowly because moisture trapped inside expands as steam. If the surface seals before the interior dries, the steam cracks the tile from within.
statCheck.fail: Tiles cracked in the kiln. Something about the heat, I'd guess.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has the best-organized storage he's ever seen. Everything labeled, stacked neatly, easy to find.
details: He said one person took it on themselves to reorganize everything. Took weeks but the result speaks for itself.
People who used to spend minutes searching for things now find them in seconds.
reactions: Organization isn't exciting work, but it multiplies every other kind of work.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has been dealing with a persistent leak in their meeting hall. Fixed once, leaks again in a different spot.
details: A carter said they've patched it four times. The water always finds a new path.
People have started bringing cloths to sit on during meetings. Acceptance is a stage of grief.
reactions: A roof that keeps finding ways to leak is a roof that needs replacing, not patching.

statCheck.pass: When a roof leaks in different spots, it usually means the underlying structure is sagging. The thatch or shingles separate where the frame dips. You fix the frame, not the surface.
statCheck.fail: Leaky roofs are persistent. I'd just keep patching and hope.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a mother who teaches all the settlement's children their letters. Does it for free, every afternoon.
details: A trader said the children practice with sticks in sand. Simple tools, but the learning is real.
Parents are grateful. A settlement where children can read trades better and plans better.
reactions: Teaching without expecting payment is the kind of contribution that changes a settlement's future.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has been overproducing {resource_type} and can't move it fast enough. Stockpiles growing.
details: He said the market is saturated. Every trader already has enough and doesn't want more.
Good production with bad timing means the effort goes to waste.
reactions: Making more than people need isn't productivity. It's wishful thinking.

statCheck.pass: Oversupply drops prices, but storing the surplus for a lean season elsewhere could turn it into leverage. The trick is finding someone who'll need it later.
statCheck.fail: Too much of something is a problem I don't have advice for.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a cat that sits on the warmest stone in the settlement every afternoon. Moves as the sun moves.
details: A peddler said the cat tracks the warm spots with perfect accuracy. Never wrong.
People joke that if you want to know where the sun's hitting, follow the cat.
reactions: Animals understand comfort in ways we overthink. The cat doesn't need a reason.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a tradition of ringing a bell three times when a trader arrives. Lets everyone know goods are in.
details: A carter said people appear from every direction within minutes of the bell. Practiced efficiency.
The best bargains go fast. Being first to the trader matters.
reactions: Simple systems for communication make a settlement run smoother than any amount of shouting.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said they've been having trouble sleeping because of frogs in the nearby pond. Deafening at night.
details: He said the chorus starts at dusk and doesn't stop until dawn. Hundreds of frogs.
People there stuff cloth in their ears. It helps a little.
reactions: Living near water has its advantages and its frogs. You get both.

statCheck.pass: Frogs are loudest during mating season, which passes in a few weeks. The rest of the year they're quiet. Visitors always seem to arrive during the worst of it.
statCheck.fail: Frogs are noisy near water. I don't think there's a fix for that.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a girl who draws maps in the dirt showing traders the best routes between settlements. Surprisingly accurate.
details: A merchant said her distances are close to right. She asks every traveler she meets about their route and memorizes it.
Traders consult her before setting out. A child giving directions to grown men, and they listen.
reactions: A mind that collects and organizes information naturally is a rare thing.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has had three different people claim to grow the largest turnip this season. Nobody wants to back down.
details: A peddler said the turnips are all roughly the same size. The differences are trivial.
The argument has become more entertaining than the turnips themselves.
reactions: Competitive pride over root vegetables is the most harmless kind of vanity.

entries.intro: A carter said {other_settlement} has an apple tree that produces fruit so sour nobody eats them fresh. But they make the best vinegar anyone's tasted.
details: He said the vinegar is sharp and clean. Perfect for preserving and cooking.
What seemed like a useless tree turned into one of their best trade goods.
reactions: Something's only useless if you haven't found the right use for it yet.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a season where every jar of preserves they made fermented instead of setting. Lids popping in the night.
details: A trader said the sound of lids popping woke people up. Every few hours, another jar failed.
The fruit was too ripe when they jarred it. A day or two earlier would have been fine.
reactions: Timing with preserves is unforgiving. A day late and you've made something nobody wants.

statCheck.pass: Overripe fruit has more wild yeast on the skin and more sugar that's already converting. Jar it too late and fermentation overwhelms the seal. The window between ripe and too-ripe is narrow.
statCheck.fail: Preserves going bad sounds wasteful. I don't do much canning myself.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a couple who disagree about everything in public and agree about everything in private. The whole settlement knows it.
details: A merchant said they argue at the well, at the fire, at the table. Then go home and laugh about it.
People find it confusing. The couple finds it entertaining.
reactions: Some couples perform their disagreements. The real relationship happens behind closed doors.

entries.intro: A peddler told me {other_settlement} has built a stone wall around their grain stores. Sturdy and rat-proof.
details: He said the wall is thick enough that nothing chews through. Even the most persistent rats give up.
The effort was significant, but they haven't lost grain to pests since.
reactions: Solving a problem permanently costs more up front and less in every year after.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a man who talks in his sleep loudly enough to be heard outside. Full conversations with nobody.
details: A carter said the man's wife puts a pillow over her own head and sleeps through it.
Neighbors sometimes hear bits and pieces. Grocery lists, arguments with people who aren't there.
reactions: A sleeping mind with a loud mouth is a combination that punishes everyone nearby.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} ran out of salt during the preserving season. Had to trade at terrible rates to get more in time.
details: A trader said they underestimated how much they'd need and the nearest supply was far.
The salt arrived just in time, but the price was twice what it should have been. Desperation costs.
reactions: Running out of something essential at the worst moment is a failure of planning that feels like bad luck.

statCheck.pass: Salt consumption follows the harvest calendar, and it's predictable. Buying a year's supply in advance when prices are low is the simplest insurance against shortage.
statCheck.fail: Running out of salt at preserving time sounds like a nightmare. Not much more I can add.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} has a woman who keeps a fire burning in the meeting hall all winter. Tends it day and night.
details: He said the fire never goes out from first frost to last. She feeds it every few hours.
People come and go throughout the day to warm up. The hall is the warmest spot in the settlement.
reactions: Tending a fire for months is thankless work that everyone depends on.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a donkey break its tether and wander into someone's garden. Ate the lettuce and trampled the beans.
details: A peddler said the donkey looked very satisfied with itself by the time they caught it.
The garden owner and the donkey owner aren't speaking. The donkey continues to eat without remorse.
reactions: Livestock damage to gardens is the oldest source of neighbor conflict in any settlement.

statCheck.pass: Donkeys chew through rope tethers if they're bored. A chain or a hobble prevents the escape, but a bored donkey will find other trouble regardless.
statCheck.fail: Donkeys get loose. That's a problem for the owner to solve.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} found that their well water rises and falls with the seasons. Full in spring, low in late summer.
details: A carter said the pattern is consistent year after year. They can predict the level by the month.
Knowing the pattern helps them plan. They draw extra in spring and store it.
reactions: Understanding your water supply's rhythm is practical knowledge that prevents panic.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said they've been experimenting with new ways to smoke {resource_type}. The flavor is getting better each batch.
details: He said they switched wood types and adjusted the smoking time. Small changes, big difference.
People who tasted the latest batch want to buy in bulk. Demand is outpacing supply.
reactions: Improving a process through trial and error is how settlements build a name for themselves.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a dog that barks at every trader who arrives and won't stop until they leave. Every single time.
details: A merchant said the dog is otherwise friendly. It just doesn't like strangers walking through with carts.
Traders have started tossing the dog a scrap of food on arrival. It stops barking while it eats.
reactions: A barking dog is a nuisance you can bribe. That makes it a manageable nuisance.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a shed that leans so far to one side that people call it the tilting house. Still standing, though.
details: A peddler said the lean gets a little worse each year. One strong wind and it's done.
Nobody stores anything important in it anymore. Just things they wouldn't mind losing.
reactions: A building that's been about to fall for years develops a kind of defiant personality.

entries.intro: A carter said {other_settlement} recently traded for a proper set of metalworking tools. First time they've had them.
details: He said the tools aren't fancy, but they work. Basic repairs no longer require a trip to another settlement.
Independence in small repairs saves more time than people realize.
reactions: Every tool a settlement acquires is one less reason to depend on someone else.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a dispute over water rights at a shared stream. Both sides claim they were there first.
details: A trader said the upstream side takes more than their share and the downstream side complains about low flow.
Nobody wants to back down. Water disputes bring out the worst in otherwise reasonable people.
reactions: Sharing water requires trust, and trust is always in shorter supply than water.

statCheck.pass: Upstream users always have leverage because gravity is on their side. The only fair system is measured allocation. Agreed amounts at agreed times. Without structure, the upstream side always wins.
statCheck.fail: Water arguments are hard to resolve. I don't have the answer.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has someone who makes candles that burn twice as long as the usual kind. Steady, clean flame.
details: A merchant said the secret is the wick material and how tightly it's braided.
Long-burning candles mean longer evenings. More work gets done, more stories get told.
reactions: An extra hour of light every evening adds up over a winter. Small improvement, large effect.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has been losing eggs to crows. The birds wait until the chickens leave the nest and swoop in.
details: He said they've tried covering the nests, but the crows figure out the covers within a day.
The cleverness of the crows is almost admirable. Almost.
reactions: Crows are smarter than they have any right to be. Outsmarting them takes serious effort.

statCheck.pass: Crows learn patterns, so the key is breaking routines. Collecting eggs at random times instead of on a schedule means the crows can't predict when the nests are unguarded.
statCheck.fail: Crows stealing eggs is frustrating. I don't know how to outsmart a bird.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a tradition of singing together while they work the fields. Different songs for different tasks.
details: A carter said the songs set the pace. A planting song is slow and steady, a harvest song is quick.
Visitors hear the singing from the road before they see the settlement.
reactions: Working in rhythm together is faster than working separately. The singing isn't decoration. It's coordination.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has an old gate that swings shut on its own and catches people by surprise. Hits them in the back.
details: A trader said the hinges are stiff and the gate is heavy. It swings with force.
People who've been hit once learn to step through quickly. Visitors always get caught.
reactions: A gate that punishes slowness is a quirk that becomes a rite of passage.

entries.intro: A merchant mentioned {other_settlement} has been sending runners between settlements to coordinate trade schedules. Saves wasted trips.
details: He said knowing what each settlement needs before the trader arrives means less guesswork.
The runners are young and fast. A round trip that takes a cart a day takes them half a morning.
reactions: Information traveling faster than goods is the foundation of efficient trade.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had their clothesline collapse while everything was drying. Clean laundry in the dirt.
details: A peddler said the post gave way and the whole line dropped. A full morning's washing ruined.
They had to do it all again. Washing clothes twice in one day is punishment.
reactions: Small failures on a laundry day feel bigger than they are. The frustration is disproportionate.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has someone who collects different types of soil and can tell you which ones grow what best. A soil expert, of all things.
details: A carter said the person has jars of soil lined up, labeled by color and texture.
Farmers consult them before planting in new ground. The advice has been reliable.
reactions: Knowing your soil is knowing your future harvests. Most people look at dirt and see nothing.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} had their only saw break during a building project. Work stopped cold.
details: He said they tried cutting with axes for a day and gave up. A saw does what an axe can't.
A replacement had to come from three settlements away. A week of waiting.
reactions: Dependence on a single tool is a vulnerability you don't notice until it breaks.

statCheck.pass: Saw blades snap when they bind in the cut. Keeping the kerf open with a wedge behind the blade prevents the stress that causes the break.
statCheck.fail: Saws break. Having only one is a gamble.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has an old couple who sit outside every morning and watch the sun come up. Rain or shine, they're there.
details: A merchant said they've done it for years. Two chairs, two cups, one sunrise.
The settlement sets its pace by them. When the old couple goes inside, the day has begun.
reactions: A small ritual shared by two people can anchor an entire settlement's morning.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has had trouble with their compost attracting wasps. Aggressive ones, right near the kitchen area.
details: A peddler said the wasps go for the fruit scraps. People approach the compost pile at a sprint.
Moving the pile would help, but nobody wants to be the one to disturb a wasp nest in the process.
reactions: Wasps turn a simple chore into a hazard. They ruin everything they nest near.

statCheck.pass: Wasps are attracted to fermenting fruit sugars. Covering food scraps with a layer of brown material like leaves or sawdust immediately reduces the smell that draws them.
statCheck.fail: Wasps are awful and I stay away from them. That's my only advice.

entries.intro: A carter told me {other_settlement} has a tradition of marking the height of each child on a doorframe every birthday. Generations of marks.
details: He said the oldest marks are from people now grown with children of their own.
The doorframe is a history of the settlement written in notches. Nobody would dare replace it.
reactions: A record of growth is a record of time. The simplest ones feel the most real.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has been getting fewer traders lately. The ones who still come say the route isn't worth the effort.
details: A merchant said the settlement doesn't produce enough to justify the travel time.
Fewer visitors means fewer goods and higher prices for what does arrive.
reactions: A settlement that traders skip over slowly falls behind. Connection is survival.

statCheck.pass: If they offered lodging and a meal to traders as part of the deal, more would come despite the thin margins. The route becomes worth it if the stopover is comfortable.
statCheck.fail: Traders go where the profit is. I can't blame them.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a woman who dyes cloth using wildflowers and bark. The colors are soft but they last.
details: A trader said the yellows and greens are especially good. Gentle on the eye, not garish.
She experiments with new plants each season. Every batch is slightly different.
reactions: Making beauty from what grows wild is a talent that costs nothing but time and attention.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has a stone path between the well and the meeting hall that stays dry even in heavy rain. Smart construction.
details: He said the stones are slightly raised and angled so water runs off to the sides.
It took effort to lay, but now the most-walked path in the settlement is never muddy.
reactions: Solving a problem once and well beats solving it poorly every week.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a night so cold that the water in the well bucket froze solid before they could pour it. Cracked the bucket.
details: A carter said people had to melt ice to drink that morning. A slow, frustrating start to the day.
The cold passed by afternoon, but the ruined bucket was a lasting reminder.
reactions: Extreme cold turns ordinary things into emergencies. A frozen bucket is a crisis when you're thirsty.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a man who repairs shoes so well that the repaired ones last longer than new ones. People damage them on purpose just to bring them to him.
details: A merchant said the man reinforces weak spots and adds material where the original cobbler skimped.
A repaired shoe from him outlasts two new pairs. Word has spread beyond the settlement.
reactions: A repair that improves on the original is the highest form of craft.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said their cheese-making this season has been inconsistent. Good one day, terrible the next.
details: He said the same person uses the same method each time. Something's different that they can't identify.
The bad batches are sour and crumbly. The good ones are perfect. No pattern to it.
reactions: Inconsistency without a clear cause is the most maddening kind of problem.

statCheck.pass: Temperature fluctuations during curing cause inconsistency. If their storage space heats up during warm days and cools at night, the cheese develops differently each time.
statCheck.fail: Cheese is temperamental. I eat it, I don't understand it.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a child who asks every visitor the same question. Where's the most beautiful place you've ever been.
details: A peddler said the child listens carefully to every answer and remembers them all.
Some travelers are caught off guard by the question. It makes them think in ways they don't expect.
reactions: A question that makes a grown person stop and think is more valuable than most conversations.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} tried to build a bridge over a narrow stream and underestimated the spring flow. The bridge washed out within a month.
details: A carter said the planks held fine in dry weather. The first real rain proved them wrong.
They're planning a second attempt with stone supports. Learning from failure.
reactions: Building for fair weather is building for half the year. The other half is what tests you.

statCheck.pass: Spring melt can triple a stream's flow. A bridge needs to account for peak flow, not average flow. Stone footings sunk below the waterline are the minimum for a crossing that lasts.
statCheck.fail: Bridges washing out isn't unusual. Water is stronger than wood.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said they've been eating a lot of dried fish lately. Fresh food hasn't been arriving as often.
details: He said the dried fish is fine but people are getting tired of it. Variety matters more than you'd think.
They've started trading with anyone who brings fresh goods, even at unfavorable rates.
reactions: Eating the same thing every day wears you down in ways that have nothing to do with hunger.

statCheck.pass: If fresh food stopped arriving, it means traders changed their route or their usual supplier dried up. Either way, someone else is buying what used to come their direction.
statCheck.fail: Dried fish is better than no fish. They'll sort it out eventually.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a well that draws the coldest water anyone's ever tasted. Visitors always comment on it.
details: A peddler said you can feel it in your teeth. Cold enough to ache if you drink too fast.
People fill extra skins just for the walk home. The water stays cool for hours.
reactions: Good water is one of those things you don't appreciate until you've had bad water.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} lost half their herb garden to a late frost. Just when everything was sprouting.
details: A carter said the frost came overnight with no warning. By morning the leaves were black.
They're replanting what they can, but the growing season is shorter now.
reactions: A frost at the wrong time is worse than a whole bad winter. At least in winter you're prepared.

statCheck.pass: Covering seedlings with straw or cloth on cold nights traps heat rising from the soil. It doesn't take much to keep a frost from settling on leaves.
statCheck.fail: Frost kills plants. Not much to be done about the weather.

entries.intro: A merchant mentioned {other_settlement} has someone who carves wooden spoons so well that people collect them. Each one different.
details: He said the carver uses whatever wood is available and lets the grain guide the shape.
People argue over which design is best. The carver doesn't seem to have a favorite.
reactions: Making something beautiful out of something ordinary is its own kind of talent.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a dispute over who owns a particular apple tree that grows on the boundary between two lots.
details: A trader said both families pick from it and both claim they planted it. Nobody remembers the truth.
The tree produces enough for both, but that's not really the point for either family.
reactions: People will argue about principle long after the practical issue has been solved.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has been doing well with their {resource_type} trade. More buyers than they can keep up with.
details: A carter said the quality has been consistent, which is what draws repeat buyers.
They've had to turn some people away. Not enough supply to meet demand.
reactions: Having more buyers than goods is the best kind of problem to have.

statCheck.pass: High demand and limited supply means they could raise prices and still sell everything. If they're smart about it, this season's surplus funds next season's expansion.
statCheck.fail: Good trade is good trade. Not much more to say about it.

entries.intro: A peddler told me {other_settlement} has a dog that greets every visitor at the edge of town. Friendly, but insistent on being petted.
details: He said the dog walks alongside you until you scratch its ears, then trots off satisfied.
Travelers who've been there before look forward to it. The dog remembers them.
reactions: A friendly dog at the gate says more about a settlement than any welcome sign.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has been having trouble keeping their fences standing. The ground shifts after every heavy rain.
details: A merchant said they've rebuilt the same stretch three times this season.
The posts hold fine in dry weather but lean over the moment the soil gets wet.
reactions: Soft ground under a fence post is a problem that only gets worse with time.

statCheck.pass: Setting posts in gravel instead of bare soil solves it. The gravel drains water away and doesn't shift when wet. More digging upfront, but you only do it once.
statCheck.fail: Fences and rain don't get along. I know that much.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has an old man who remembers every trade deal the settlement has ever made. Decades of transactions, all in his head.
details: A trader said the man can recall exact quantities and prices from years ago.
People consult him before making deals to see what similar goods have gone for in the past.
reactions: A sharp memory is worth more than a ledger when the ledger gets lost.

statCheck.pass: A man like that holds power whether he means to or not. If he says a price is fair people believe him, and if he says it's unfair the deal falls through.
statCheck.fail: Good memory is useful. I wish mine were half as sharp.

entries.intro: A carter said {other_settlement} has been dealing with a well that gives muddy water after storms. Takes days to clear up.
details: He said people collect rainwater in barrels as a backup, but it's not ideal.
The well is fine most of the time. It's the few days after rain that cause problems.
reactions: Clean water is something you take for granted until you don't have it for a few days.

statCheck.pass: The well casing probably has cracks below the waterline where runoff seeps through the stonework and carries sediment in. Resealing the casing with clay would fix it.
statCheck.fail: Muddy well water after rain sounds unpleasant. I suppose they manage.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has started a communal garden where anyone can plant and anyone can harvest. It's working surprisingly well.
details: A peddler said most people plant more than they take. Pride in contributing, maybe.
There were doubts at first that people would be fair about it. Turns out most people are.
reactions: Trust is easier to build than people think. You just have to give someone the chance.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a chimney that smokes badly no matter what they burn. The whole building fills up.
details: A merchant said the smoke backs up when the wind comes from the east. People cough through dinner.
They've tried different fuels and different fire sizes. Nothing helps.
reactions: A bad chimney makes an entire building miserable. You can't ignore smoke.

statCheck.pass: The flue is probably too short or too wide. A chimney needs enough height to create a strong draft, and a narrow flue pulls air better than a wide one. Extending it a few feet would likely fix the draw.
statCheck.fail: Smoky chimneys are a nuisance. I'm glad it's not my problem.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} mentioned they've been having beautiful sunsets lately. Something about the dust in the air.
details: He said the whole sky turns orange and pink. People stop what they're doing to watch.
It's been going on for weeks. Nobody's sure why, but nobody's complaining either.
reactions: Sometimes the best thing about a place is something nobody planned or built.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had a roof beam crack during a meal. Scared everyone, but nobody was hurt.
details: A carter said the crack was loud enough to make people jump out of their seats.
The beam held together well enough to last the night, but they replaced it the next morning.
reactions: A cracking beam is a warning. At least it warned them before it failed completely.

statCheck.pass: Wood beams crack when they dry unevenly or bear too much weight for their span. If the beam was green when installed, it shrank as it dried and the stress built up over time.
statCheck.fail: Old wood breaks sometimes. Better it cracked than collapsed.

entries.intro: I heard someone in {other_settlement} figured out how to keep bread fresh an extra day by wrapping it in damp cloth. Small thing, big difference.
details: A peddler said the whole settlement does it now. Less waste, less baking.
The trick is the cloth has to be barely damp. Too wet and the bread goes soggy.
reactions: The most useful discoveries are usually the simplest ones.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has a family that argues loudly enough for the whole settlement to hear. Every evening, like clockwork.
details: He said the neighbors have gotten used to it. They can tell the time by the shouting.
Nobody intervenes because the family always makes up by morning. It's just how they communicate.
reactions: Some families run on friction. It works for them, even if the neighbors suffer.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} traded a good quantity of {resource_type} for salt last month. Smart move before winter.
details: A carter said they got a fair rate. The salt merchant was happy to take what they offered.
Salt stores well and you always need more than you think when the cold sets in.
reactions: Planning ahead with your surplus is the difference between a comfortable winter and a hard one.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a cat that catches more mice than any trap. The whole settlement depends on one cat.
details: A trader said the cat patrols the grain stores like it understands the job.
People feed it well and it sleeps wherever it likes. Fair trade, really.
reactions: A working cat earns its keep better than most people realize.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has a path that turns into a stream when it rains. People wade through it in bare feet.
details: He said the water runs ankle-deep through the middle of the settlement during storms.
They've dug ditches alongside it, but the water ignores the ditches and follows the path.
reactions: Water goes where it wants. You can work with it or against it, but it always wins.

statCheck.pass: The path sits in a natural drainage channel where compacted ground keeps water from soaking in. Laying gravel and crowning the path higher in the center would push water to the ditches.
statCheck.fail: Rain and paths don't mix well. I don't envy them.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a woman who makes the strongest rope anyone's seen. Traders buy it specifically from her.
details: A merchant said she twists the fibers tighter than anyone else and uses a method she won't share.
The rope costs more but lasts so long that it's cheaper in the end.
reactions: Quality work finds its own market. People remember who makes things that last.

statCheck.pass: She probably soaks the fibers before twisting, which lets them lock together more tightly as they dry. The tension holds because the material shrank around itself.
statCheck.fail: Strong rope is strong rope. I wouldn't know why one is better than another.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a batch of preserved food go bad early. The seal wasn't right on the jars.
details: A carter said the wax they used was too thin and cracked in the heat.
Half a season's worth of preserves lost. They're making do with what's left.
reactions: Losing preserved food is losing twice. The food and the time that went into preparing it.

statCheck.pass: Wax needs to be layered thick enough that temperature changes don't crack it. Two coats with drying time between them seals better than one thick pour.
statCheck.fail: Bad seals spoil food. That's a hard lesson.

entries.intro: A trader mentioned {other_settlement} has the friendliest people he's met on his whole route. Everyone waves, everyone talks.
details: He said a stranger there gets treated like a neighbor within an hour.
They offered him a meal before he even had a chance to set up his goods.
reactions: Hospitality like that makes a place memorable. It costs nothing and buys everything.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has been quiet lately. Fewer travelers passing through, less noise, less everything.
details: A peddler said the place felt sleepy. Not abandoned, just slow.
People were going about their business, but there wasn't much business to go about.
reactions: A quiet settlement isn't necessarily a troubled one. Sometimes things just slow down.

statCheck.pass: Quiet spells often follow a bad trade season. If the settlement's main product didn't sell well, everything else slows with it. They're probably tightening belts and waiting for things to turn.
statCheck.fail: Quiet places make me uneasy, but that might just be me.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a man who tells the same three stories to every visitor. Word for word, every time.
details: A merchant said the stories aren't even interesting. But the man tells them with such conviction that you listen anyway.
Regular visitors know the stories by heart and mouth the words along with him.
reactions: Repetition is comforting, I suppose. The man and his stories are part of the place now.

entries.intro: A carter told me {other_settlement} has a field that grows better crops on one side than the other. Same soil, same seeds, different results.
details: He said the east side of the field produces twice what the west side does.
They've tried switching seeds and it doesn't matter. The east side just grows better.
reactions: Soil has its own logic. Sometimes you just work with what it gives you.

statCheck.pass: One side probably gets more morning sun and stays warmer earlier in the day. Plants on that side start growing sooner each morning and accumulate the advantage over weeks.
statCheck.fail: Farming is full of mysteries like that. Could be anything.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has been losing livestock to illness this year. One after another, no clear pattern.
details: A peddler said the animals look thin and listless. Nobody can figure out the cause.
They've separated the sick ones from the healthy, but the illness keeps spreading.
reactions: Losing animals one by one is worse than losing them all at once. The dread of who's next wears you down.

statCheck.pass: If the illness is passing from animal to animal, it's likely in the shared water supply. Cleaning the trough and using fresh water from a different source would tell them quickly.
statCheck.fail: Sick livestock is bad news. I hope they figure it out before they lose more.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a stretch of bad sleep going around. Half the settlement tossing and turning for no clear reason.
details: A trader said people blamed the heat, the food, the moon, each other.
It passed after a week or so. Nobody figured out the cause.
reactions: Bad sleep spreads like a mood. One person yawning makes the whole settlement tired.

statCheck.pass: Shared insomnia often traces to a shared cause. A change in water source, a seasonal plant blooming nearby, or even a shift in routine that throws everyone off at once.
statCheck.fail: Bad sleep happens. Could be anything.

entries.intro: A peddler said {other_settlement} has someone who makes candles that burn twice as long as normal. Uses a different kind of wick.
details: He said the trick is a tighter braid on the wick. Burns slower, less drip.
People trade for those candles specifically. A long-burning candle is worth more than you'd think.
reactions: Light after dark is one of those everyday luxuries that doesn't feel like a luxury until you don't have it.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a door that won't stay closed. Swings open on its own no matter how many times they fix the latch.
details: A carter said they've replaced the hinges twice and the frame once. Still swings open.
People have given up and just prop a stone against it. Stubbornness has its limits.
reactions: Some problems refuse to be solved. You learn to live around them.

statCheck.pass: The doorframe is probably warped from the building settling unevenly, so the door swings toward whichever side gravity favors. Rehang it plumb to the frame, not to the building.
statCheck.fail: Doors have minds of their own sometimes. A good stone solves most problems.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a woman who knows every edible plant within walking distance. People bring her things to identify.
details: A merchant said she can tell safe from dangerous by smell alone.
The knowledge was passed down from her mother, who learned it the same way.
reactions: That kind of knowledge is worth more than most people realize. You can't learn it from a book.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said their grain stores are lower than usual this year. Not dangerously low, but noticeable.
details: He said the harvest was fine but demand was higher than expected.
They're being careful with portions and making things stretch.
reactions: A thin margin between enough and not enough makes people careful. That's not the worst thing.

statCheck.pass: If they're short on grain but have surplus of something else, a trade before winter would even things out. Waiting until they're desperate means worse terms.
statCheck.fail: Low stores are worrying but they'll manage. People always do.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has let their paths go to ruin. Mud, refuse, nobody cleans up after themselves.
details: A peddler said he tracked muck through his own cart after walking through the place.
People used to keep things tidy there. Something changed and nobody bothered to keep the standard.
reactions: Once cleanliness stops being a habit, the decline is fast. Nobody wants to be the first to start sweeping again.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has been getting more birds than usual. Nesting on every roof and ledge.
details: A carter said the noise starts before dawn and doesn't stop until dark.
The droppings are the real problem. Everything outdoors needs covering.
reactions: Birds are charming in small numbers. In large numbers they're a nuisance.

statCheck.pass: More birds usually means more insects, which is what they eat. The bird population follows the food supply, so the real change happened at the insect level first.
statCheck.fail: Birds go where they want. I don't know why there'd be more of them.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} has a child who draws pictures in the dirt with a stick. Surprisingly good ones.
details: He said the child draws animals, faces, buildings. All from memory.
People step around the drawings to avoid scuffing them. Rain washes them away, and the child starts over.
reactions: Talent shows up where you least expect it. The medium doesn't matter.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} had an argument about whether to expand the grain storage or build a new pen for livestock. Couldn't agree.
details: A trader said both sides had good points but nobody was willing to compromise.
They ended up doing neither and putting it off until next season.
reactions: Indecision is its own kind of decision. Usually the wrong one.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a woman who sings while she works. Not to anyone in particular, just to herself.
details: A peddler said you can hear her from the other side of the settlement on quiet mornings.
She doesn't take requests and she doesn't stop if she notices you listening.
reactions: Someone who sings while they work is doing all right. The music tells you that.

entries.intro: A carter told me {other_settlement} has had trouble with their bread lately. Dense, heavy loaves that don't rise properly.
details: He said the baker blames the flour, the miller blames the wheat, and the farmer blames the soil.
Everyone's pointing at someone else, and the bread is still bad.
reactions: A chain of blame that goes in a circle never fixes anything.

statCheck.pass: If the yeast culture went bad, that would explain everything regardless of flour quality. A fresh starter would tell them in one batch whether the problem is the yeast or the grain.
statCheck.fail: Bad bread is bad bread. I hope they sort it out.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has had things going missing from people's homes. Small items, nothing valuable, but it's unsettling.
details: A merchant said people started locking up at night, which they never used to do.
Trust erodes quickly when you can't account for your own belongings.
reactions: Losing trust in your neighbors over missing spoons is petty, but the feeling is real.

statCheck.pass: It's probably a child or an animal getting in. People assume the worst, but most petty theft in small settlements turns out to be mundane.
statCheck.fail: Things going missing is never comfortable. I'd lock my door too.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has a well-worn path between two houses that people use as a shortcut. The residents gave up trying to stop it.
details: A peddler said the homeowners used to put obstacles in the way. People just walked around them.
Eventually they put down stepping stones and accepted the situation.
reactions: People find the fastest route and no amount of effort will change their minds.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} has been burning through {resource_type} faster than expected. They'll need to trade for more before long.
details: He said a bigger population means bigger consumption. Simple as that.
They haven't run out yet, but the margin is thinner than they'd like.
reactions: Growing faster than your supplies can keep up is a problem disguised as success.

statCheck.pass: If they negotiate trades now while they still have a surplus of something else, they'll get better terms. Waiting until they're desperate means paying whatever's asked.
statCheck.fail: Running low on supplies is never comfortable. They'll figure it out.

entries.intro: Word is {other_settlement} has a man who can tell what the weather will do three days out just by watching the clouds. He's right more often than not.
details: A carter said the man reads cloud shapes and wind direction like most people read faces.
Farmers there plan their work around his predictions. Saves them grief.
reactions: Practical wisdom is invisible until you need it. Then it's the most valuable thing around.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} had a fence collapse and let all their chickens loose. Took three days to round them all up.
details: A merchant said the chickens scattered in every direction. Some ended up on rooftops.
The last one was found roosting inside someone's storage shed, perfectly content.
reactions: Chickens are faster and more determined than anyone gives them credit for.
